title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “Meta-PAC: High-Probability Prediction Sets for Few-Shot Meta-Learning”

Summary  
This paper proposes Meta-PAC, a principled extension of PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting. Building on PS-BINOM’s per-task calibration via Clopper–Pearson (CP) confidence intervals, the authors introduce a two-step, meta-level binomial PAC procedure that aggregates task-specific thresholds into a single global threshold. This construction yields high-probability coverage guarantees uniformly over new tasks, in contrast to existing Meta-CP methods which only ensure marginal coverage on the (n+1)th task. Empirically, Meta-PAC is validated on few-shot vision, NLP, and medical datasets, demonstrating coverage close to the nominal level and competitive set sizes.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical novelty and rigor: The paper extends classical PAC prediction sets into the meta-learning regime with a clean two-stage calibration that avoids the usual O(1/N) simultaneous-coverage correction by exploiting the i.i.d. nature of per-task thresholds.  
2. Sample-efficient calibration: By splitting the α level into two halves (α/2 per stage) instead of paying an α/N penalty, the authors achieve tighter, more sample-efficient confidence bounds.  
3. Empirical validation: Thorough experiments on vision, language, and medical few-shot benchmarks support the claimed high-probability guarantees and demonstrate practical utility.  
4. Meaningful extension: The framework subsumes previous Meta-CP approaches and offers a conceptually appealing way to transfer PAC coverage guarantees across tasks.  
5. Strong results: Coverage and average set sizes across tasks confirm that Meta-PAC behaves as predicted by theory, with only modest conservatism.

Weaknesses  
1. Presentation and readability: The notation is dense, and the paper jumps quickly into formalism without an intuitive overview of the algorithm or its goals.  
2. Static threshold: Meta-PAC outputs a single global threshold for all downstream tasks, ignoring task-specific difficulty or available data size.  
3. Clopper–Pearson tightness: It remains unclear how conservative the CP intervals are when individual calibration sets S_i are small, potentially leading to overly large prediction sets.  
4. Heavy assumptions: The method requires access to hundreds of i.i.d. calibration tasks, which may be unrealistic in many real-world domains.  
5. Incremental novelty: While the combination of Meta-CP and PAC is well motivated, the individual components are largely existing techniques, so the overall impact may be moderate.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• High-level algorithm sketch (Section 4): Before diving into symbols, include a concise, step-by-step description of Meta-PAC—its inputs, calibration stages, and final threshold selection—to help readers grasp the big picture.  
• Clarify PS-BINOM details (Lines 171–174): The role of the dummy label and the function g(τ,y)=τ·1[y=1] would benefit from a clearer, more intuitive explanation.  
• Adaptive thresholds: Discuss how one might adjust the global threshold on a per-task basis using adaptation data (e.g., early stopping, local CP intervals) to better handle easy vs. hard tasks.  
• Limitations section: Expand your discussion of the strong assumptions—particularly the need for many calibration tasks and average-task guarantees—so practitioners can assess applicability to their own settings.  
• Empirical tightness study: Provide either theoretical bounds or additional experiments that illustrate the tightness of the CP intervals when n is small, to guide users in choosing calibration set sizes.

Recommendation  
Overall, Meta-PAC makes a technically solid contribution to the theory and practice of high-confidence prediction in meta-learning. Despite presentation and practical assumptions that limit immediacy of adoption, the paper advances our understanding of transferring PAC guarantees across tasks. I therefore recommend acceptance as a weak accept (6).

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the extension of PAC prediction sets to few-shot meta-learning both technically solid and practically useful, noting that Meta-PAC sets offer stronger high-probability coverage guarantees across tasks than prior Meta-CP methods. They praise the empirical validation but raise concerns about readability, the static threshold design, and the reliance on large calibration task collections.
strengths: Clear theoretical contribution: extends PAC prediction sets to meta-learning with stronger coverage guarantees than Meta-CP
Calibration strategy is correct and more sample-efficient (avoids a 1/N factor via two 1/2 factors)
Well-designed experiments demonstrating empirical validity across vision, language, and medical tasks
Theoretical setting is appealing and a meaningful extension of previous methods
Empirical results are strong and support the claimed PAC guarantees
weaknesses: Presentation is notation-heavy and difficult to follow; lacks a high-level description of the algorithm before diving into symbols
Uses a single global threshold for all downstream tasks, without adapting it to the difficulty or data size of each new task
Unclear how tight Clopper–Pearson bounds are when calibration task sample sizes are small
Assumes availability of several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks, which may be unrealistic in many settings
Novelty is somewhat incremental, combining existing Meta-CP and PAC techniques, so overall impact may be modest
suggestions: At the start of Section 4, add a concise, step-by-step high-level description of the Meta-PAC goal and algorithm before introducing formal notation
Clarify the role of the function g and the dummy label in the PS-BINOM procedure (Lines 171–174)
Discuss potential methods to adapt the threshold per test task based on observed adaptation data
Expand the discussion of limitations to emphasize the strong assumptions (e.g., many calibration tasks, average-task guarantees)
Provide guidance or empirical exploration on the tightness of Clopper–Pearson bounds in low-sample regimes
main_points: Meta-PAC sets provide high-probability coverage guarantees across new tasks, unlike Meta-CP which only yields marginal coverage over the (n+1)th task
The two-step calibration (per-task PAC thresholds followed by a meta-level PAC interval) avoids an O(1/N) penalty and remains i.i.d.
Reusing a single static threshold after adaptation means the method does not exploit easy or hard test tasks differently
The notation and formalism, while precise, make the paper hard to parse without an upfront intuitive overview
The requirement of hundreds of calibration tasks and use of Clopper–Pearson intervals may limit practical applicability
technical_details: PS-BINOM uses Clopper–Pearson (binomial) confidence intervals to obtain per-task PAC thresholds ˆτ_{ε,α/2}(S_i)
Meta calibration forms an empirical sample of i.i.d. per-task thresholds, then runs a second binomial PAC procedure to get a global interval [ˆτ_{α/2,δ}^–, ˆτ_{α/2,δ}^+]
Avoids a 1/N simultaneous-coverage correction by leveraging the fact that the PAC procedure output is i.i.d. rather than simultaneously valid
Clopper–Pearson bounds can be conservative for small n, raising questions about threshold tightness when calibration sets are small
Lines 171–174 introduce a dummy label and function g(τ,y)=τ⋅1[y=1], which needs clearer explanation

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.
reviews.questions: Please refer to weaknesss above.
reviews.limitations: Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel approach to constructing PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. By first calibrating a prediction-set parameter during meta-training and then combining it with an adapted score function at test time, the method provides provable mPAC guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets. Empirically, the authors validate their approach on four benchmarks—mini-ImageNet (5-way, 5-shot), CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and the CDC Heart dataset—demonstrating both desired coverage and reduced set size compared to non-meta baselines.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The paper is very well written, with a clear problem statement, detailed methodology, and lucid proofs of the mPAC guarantee.  
2. Solid Theoretical Foundation: The authors provide a rigorous theoretical analysis showing that the adapted prediction sets satisfy PAC guarantees inherited from the meta-training calibration.  
3. Broad Empirical Evaluation: Experiments span multiple tasks and modalities. The use of four diverse datasets lends confidence that the method generalizes across classification and regression, image and tabular or relational data.

Weaknesses  
1. Fixed Prediction-Set Parameter at Adaptation: Although the score function is fine-tuned per task, the meta-learned prediction-set parameter remains unchanged after calibration. This could limit flexibility when faced with more heterogeneous tasks.  
2. Limited to Metric-Based Meta-Learning: All experiments rely on a Prototypical Network backbone. It is unclear whether the approach extends readily to optimization-based meta-learners such as Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML).  
3. Restricted Mini-ImageNet Setup: The evaluation on mini-ImageNet focuses solely on the standard 5-way classification. Harder regimes (e.g., 10-way or 20-way) are omitted, leaving open questions about performance under greater class ambiguity.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Jointly Adapt the Prediction-Set Parameter: Investigate strategies to update the prediction-set calibration parameter along with the score function during test-time adaptation, potentially improving set size without sacrificing coverage.  
2. Extend to Optimization-Based Methods: Demonstrate the approach on MAML or related gradient-based meta-learners to highlight generality beyond metric-based architectures.  
3. Evaluate on More Challenging Settings: Add experiments on mini-ImageNet 10-way or 20-way tasks to assess how well the PAC guarantees and set-size benefits hold under increased classification difficulty.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work makes a meaningful contribution by marrying PAC prediction sets with meta-learning and backs it with sound theory and diverse experiments. While there are clear avenues for extension—most notably adapting the calibration parameter and broadening the meta-learning paradigms—the current manuscript is technically solid and of moderate-to-high impact. I therefore recommend weak acceptance.

* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents mPAC, a novel extension of PAC conformal prediction to the meta-learning setting. By recognizing that meta-learning involves sampling multiple related tasks (each with its own adaptation set) rather than a single covariate shift scenario, the authors introduce an (ε, α, δ)-mPAC notion (Definition 3). They instantiate mPAC via a concrete two-step calibration scheme (Algorithm 1): first estimating per-task PAC thresholds at error rate α/2, then aggregating these estimates in a second PAC step at target rate α/2 and confidence 1–δ to select a global threshold τ. Theoretical analysis (Theorem 1) rigorously proves that this procedure attains the desired mPAC guarantee on unseen test tasks. Presentation is clear, well organized, and the paper successfully fills an important gap by providing PAC-style uncertainty quantification in meta-learning.

Strengths  
1. Generalization of PAC conformal prediction. The paper extends classical PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning context, a natural yet nontrivial leap that addresses scenarios with multiple related tasks and adaptation sets.  
2. Concrete algorithmic proposal. Algorithm 1 is built upon well-understood PAC prediction methods, making it easy to implement and reason about.  
3. Strong theoretical backing. Theorem 1 offers a transparent and rigorous proof of the mPAC guarantee, clarifying how the two-step calibration yields correct coverage with high probability.  
4. Clarity of exposition. Definitions, algorithms, and proofs are laid out in a logical order, and the overall narrative is accessible.  
5. Important practical impact. Providing PAC-style uncertainty estimates for meta-learning tasks addresses a clear need in applications where task-specific adaptation and reliability are critical.

Weaknesses  
• Computational cost of Algorithm 2. The inner PAC estimator in Algorithm 1 relies on a grid search over τ (Algorithm 2), but the paper does not analyze the grid-search’s computational burden or how the choice of granularity affects performance and coverage.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Include an empirical or theoretical study of Algorithm 2’s grid-search cost. For instance, report runtimes as a function of grid size or dataset scale to help practitioners balance accuracy and efficiency.  
2. Discuss potential strategies to accelerate or approximate the grid search. Ideas might include adaptive search schemes, coarser initial grids followed by local refinement, or sampling-based estimators of τ that reduce the number of candidate thresholds.

Conclusion  
Overall, this manuscript delivers a technically sound and well-presented extension of PAC prediction to the meta-learning regime, complete with a clear algorithm and solid theoretical guarantees. While the computational aspects of the threshold search merit further discussion, they do not detract from the paper’s core contributions. I recommend weak acceptance. Rating: 6: Weak Accept.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “Title Unknown”

Summary  
This paper develops a two-stage, training‐conditional coverage guarantee for meta-learning, leveraging a novel chaining of binomial concentration inequalities. The authors present PS-BINOM, an algorithm that calibrates prediction sets in two phases—first over the meta-training tasks and then over a per-task calibration set—and validate their approach with experiments in vision, language, and medical domains. They claim this is the first work to offer a two-stage training-conditional guarantee and only the second to apply training-conditional conformal prediction in a meta-learning setting.

Strengths  
1. The theoretical contributions are sound. The proofs of coverage and the chaining argument—using two applications of binomial concentration—are rigorous and clearly correct.  
2. Empirically, the paper delivers a broad suite of experiments across vision, language, and healthcare tasks, demonstrating consistent validity and useful set sizes.  
3. Novelty: to my knowledge, this is indeed the first paper to formulate a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, and it expands on prior work in an interesting direction.  
4. The PS-BINOM algorithm is a clever adaptation of Vovk’s Proposition 2b (from [21]) to the meta-learning context.  
5. Overall the technical and empirical pipeline is reproducible; evaluation metrics and code dependencies are sensible.

Weaknesses  
1. The core guarantee is triply-nested—an expectation of 0–1 loss inside two conditional probabilities over both the calibration set and meta-learned parameters. This complexity threatens practical adoption: it is hard to parse and may never be used by practitioners.  
2. The writing is dense, especially in Section 4.1. Key definitions and parameter roles (ε, α, β) are introduced too quickly, making it difficult for readers outside a small PAC community to follow.  
3. Figure 2 contains several unexplained elements (striped orientations, missing color legends, and an unlabeled box marked “N”), which further confuses interpretation.  
4. In terms of domain novelty, the contribution feels incremental: the two-stage guarantee is a fairly straightforward extension of existing conformal/meta-learning results.  
5. Most concerning is an attribution error: the paper repeatedly rebrands training-conditional conformal prediction as “PAC prediction sets [3]” without fully crediting Vovk [21], who first introduced training-conditional validity.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Simplify and clarify the meta-PAC guarantee. Provide intuition for the triply-nested form and concrete guidance on how to choose ε, α, and β in practice.  
• Rewrite Section 4.1 to walk the reader step by step through the guarantee. Add an illustrative example or toy scenario to demystify the nested probabilities.  
• Update Figure 2: explain each visual element, include a complete legend, and ensure all boxes and stripes are labeled.  
• Fully acknowledge Vovk [21] as the originator of training-conditional conformal prediction. Remove or correct the “PAC prediction sets [3]” phrasing and merge this reference with the discussion of PS-BINOM’s relationship to Proposition 2b in [21].  
• Add a discussion on realistic scenarios where the i.i.d. task assumption holds and offer guidance on when two-stage calibration yields significant benefits over simpler single-stage methods.

Conclusion  
This paper presents a technically solid contribution with rigorous proofs and thorough experiments. However, the triply-nested guarantee and dense presentation significantly limit accessibility and practical impact, and the misattribution of prior work is a serious oversight. I recommend a Weak Accept, contingent on the authors’ careful revision to simplify exposition, rectify attribution, and clarify real-world applicability.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel algorithm for constructing meta-learning prediction sets with a rigorous PAC guarantee. Unlike many conformal approaches that require test-time calibration, the proposed method estimates its threshold parameter τ during meta-train adaptation and calibration, ensuring the desired coverage with high probability. The authors validate their approach (using the PS-BINOM estimator for τ) on four datasets spanning vision, language, and medical domains. 

Strengths  
1. PAC Guarantee: The core technical contribution is a clear PAC bound on coverage, which is a significant advance for meta-learning prediction sets.  
2. Cross-Domain Evaluation: Empirical results on four diverse datasets demonstrate the practical viability of the method.  
3. No Test-Time Calibration: By leveraging meta-training and calibration phases, the algorithm avoids the overhead of per-task test-time tuning, which is appealing in low-data regimes.  

Weaknesses  
1. Confusing Notation: Throughout the manuscript, the notation for the two score functions (f and g) and for the prediction-set construction is hard to follow and sometimes inconsistent.  
2. Missing High-Level Overview: Figure 1 and accompanying text do not make it explicit that f is the pre-trained meta-model, leaving readers without a roadmap for the algorithm.  
3. Lack of Self-Containment: The paper leans heavily on prior work [1] (“PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction”) but does not provide enough background on conformal/PAC prediction-set theory for readers unfamiliar with those techniques.  
4. Limited Model Diversity: Experiments are restricted to the prototypical network architecture. It remains unclear whether the PAC guarantee and empirical performance translate to other meta-learning models (e.g., MAML, Relation Networks).  
5. Ambiguous Input Spaces: In pseudocode (lines 106 vs 108), the two score functions appear to take different inputs, but the text never explains why or how these domains differ.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Simplify and standardize the notation for both score functions and the prediction-set definitions to improve readability.  
– Add a high-level diagram or paragraph making clear that f is a meta-learned model and describing the overall flow from meta-train adaptation through calibration to meta-test prediction.  
– Include a brief tutorial on PAC prediction sets (e.g., Vovk’s training-conditional conformal framework) and explicitly summarize key results from [1] to make the paper more self-contained.  
– Broaden the empirical study by applying the algorithm to additional meta-learning architectures beyond prototypical networks, thus demonstrating generality.  
– Explain the differing input spaces for the score functions in lines 106 and 108, clarifying their roles in the algorithm.  
– Provide a more detailed comparison of Meta-CP versus Meta-PS in terms of both error rates and prediction-set sizes.  
– Investigate alternative estimators for τ beyond PS-BINOM to assess their impact on coverage and set efficiency.  

Recommendation  
5: Borderline accept.  
This work introduces a technically sound algorithm with a strong theoretical guarantee and promising cross-domain results. However, the paper’s presentation would benefit substantially from clearer notation, added background, and broader experimental validation. Once these issues are addressed, the contribution will be of clear interest to the meta-learning and conformal-prediction communities.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces the task of Grounded Reinforcement Learning, where an agent must maximize task reward while adhering to high-level commands. To tackle this, the authors propose RED, an iterative algorithm that alternates between pure reinforcement-learning updates (ignoring the commands) and behavior-cloning updates (leveraging demonstrations plus self-generated data). They evaluate in the MiniRTS environment, showing that existing methods (vanilla RL, constrained RL, behavior cloning, and IRL) fail to achieve both high win rates and command compliance, while RED achieves competitive performance. A human evaluation of command adherence further supports the approach.

Strengths  
1. Problem formulation: The grounded RL setup is compelling, particularly from a safety standpoint, as it formalizes the challenge of following high-level instructions in an RL context.  
2. Clarity of presentation: The paper is well written, with clear explanations of the task, the RED algorithm, and the experimental protocol.  
3. Human evaluation: Conducting a human study to assess how well the agent follows commands adds credibility beyond purely automatic metrics.  
4. Potential impact: Framing behavioral constraints via language has clear implications for safe and controllable RL systems.

Weaknesses  
1. Representation of “language”: Commands are treated as one-hot vectors, lacking any syntax, semantics, or compositional structure, which weakens the claim of true language grounding.  
2. Limited novelty of RED: The algorithm essentially alternates between two standard objectives (pure RL and behavior cloning), so the technical contribution is modest.  
3. Marginal empirical gains: While RED outperforms the baselines, the improvements in win rates and command compliance are not dramatic.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify novelty: Discuss in detail how command-conditioned RL differs from simply augmenting the state with a command vector and training a single RL agent.  
2. Opponent evaluation: Justify the chosen difficulty of the built-in MiniRTS opponent, and if possible, include results against a stronger adversary to demonstrate robustness.  
3. Linguistic structure: Explain what linguistic features, if any, the one-hot tokens capture (e.g., compositionality or pragmatic aspects).  
4. Pretrained language models: As a future direction, consider employing pretrained embeddings or language models to introduce genuine syntax and semantics into the command representation.

Recommendation: Accept (7: Technically solid paper with clear contributions, good evaluation, and potential impact on grounded RL and safety applications.)

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:

- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)

The authors provide limitation section.

reviews.text:
Review for RED: Constrained Reinforcement Learning with Demonstrations (Weak Accept)

Summary  
This paper introduces RED, a method that blends behavior cloning (BC) to leverage human demonstrations with constrained reinforcement learning (RL) to explore improved policies under user-provided commands. The core idea is to maintain a Lagrangian constraint on deviation from demonstration behavior while optimizing for task reward. The authors evaluate RED in the MiniRTS environment, reporting superior performance over baselines under sub-optimal commands, and supplement their submission with visualizations, video materials, and released code. Overall, I find the paper technically sound and potentially impactful, though certain aspects require further clarification before full publication.

Strengths  
1. The central idea is concise and well motivated: using BC as a “grounding” mechanism while allowing RL exploration to surpass demonstrator performance under changing commands.  
2. Visualizations, including Figure 1 and the supplementary videos, effectively illustrate the grounded RL workflow and policy behavior across commands.  
3. The released codebase is a valuable resource for reproducibility and follow-up work.  
4. Figure 1 in particular provides clear intuition for how RED interpolates between imitation and exploration.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited novelty: Conditional imitation learning has been explored in prior work (e.g., “End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning”), and the Lagrangian formulation here diverges from classical constrained-RL theory without a formal update description.  
2. Scope of applicability: RED assumes access to environment rewards at test time, which narrows its utility in purely offline or reward-scarce settings.  
3. Key experimental results (Table 1) are confusing. It is unclear how command-agnostic baselines produce varying outcomes when test-time commands change, and the differences between methods often fall within overlapping standard deviations.  
4. Missing methodological details: The paper does not specify the update rule for the Lagrangian multiplier β in Section 4.3, nor does it fully explain the 0/1 per-unit action selection design or how “NA” commands are inserted and encoded among a fixed command set.  
5. Documentation and reproducibility: The code release lacks in‐depth usage instructions and the supplementary video appears brief and possibly scripted, leaving questions about actual human involvement.  
6. The submission currently omits an Ethics Statement covering the human-subject components, and several ancillary materials (e.g., anonymized supplementary links) are not presented in a double-blind-friendly manner.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Include a clear, formal update rule or pseudocode for the Lagrangian multiplier β in Section 4.3, and discuss how this diverges from standard constrained-RL solvers.  
• Clarify the “0/1” selection action in the action space design: who executes it, and how it integrates with the policy logits.  
• Detail how “NA” commands are incorporated and tokenized in the command encoder when the number of possible commands is fixed.  
• Revise Table 1 presentation: explicitly state how each baseline handles test-time commands, add error bars or confidence intervals, and discuss statistical significance of observed gains.  
• Expand evaluations to include at least one simpler or alternative benchmark to demonstrate RED’s generality beyond MiniRTS.  
• Provide comprehensive documentation in the code repository, fix any broken links, and ensure compliance with double-blind review by anonymizing supplementary appendices.  
• Add a concise Ethics Statement regarding collection and use of human demonstrations.  
• Proofread the manuscript for consistent formatting and minor grammatical errors.

Conclusion  
RED proposes a technically solid and well-motivated approach for grounded RL with demonstrations, backed by clear visual illustrations and open-source code. However, questions remain around novelty, experimental clarity, and methodological detail. After addressing these points, the work will make a valuable contribution to the field. I therefore recommend a weak accept.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.

Questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)

Limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
