The paper clearly illustrated the method in detail. The authors compared their method with other methods quantitatively and qualitatively. They also evaluated their model with multiple occluding objects, showing it can reconstruct the unseen area with shading and shadow cues.
 
### Weakness

The authors studied the contribution of different components (shading and shadow) of their method. But the impact of different loss terms was not included. Specially, at L171, how the value of $\alpha$ for normal smoothness loss is found is not illustrated.

Questions:
How’s the normal smoothness loss affect your method? Is that similar to UNISURF? From your result, especially on LEGO, the loss of detail of studs and track seems very serious. Is that caused by normal smoothness loss or by more complicated light reflection at those areas?

Limitations:
Yes, the authors addressed different aspect of limitations of the work in the paper and supplementary materials. They also pointed out this method can recover some non-directly seen area, that may address some privacy issues.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

Reconstruction quality assessed on objects like LEGO, BUNNY, and CHAIR under 128 point lights

The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This paper is about using neural radiance field to reconstruction the geometry of an object lit by varying directional light sources, leveraging cues of cast shadow. The optimization uses an efficient way to trace rays for shadow computation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The setup is interesting as in using shadow cues to reconstruct 3D geometry when multi-view captures are not available. The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This highly controlled lighting setup seems to only be feasible in a laboratory or a light stage setting. This may be the reason the paper only shows toy synthetic examples. Another 'contrived' setup is in the simple scene / background. Cast shadow depends on the object geometry, the lighting, and also the surface the shadow is cast on. reviews.text:
Review for “Single-View Neural Reconstruction from Cast-Shadow Cues”

Summary  
This paper presents a single-view 3D reconstruction method that leverages cast-shadow cues under varying directional illumination. By embedding a neural radiance field and incorporating an efficient ray-tracer to compute shadow visibility along each light path, the authors optimize geometry without any multi-view captures. The core idea is to infer shape from how shadows shift under known light directions, using root-finding and occupancy sampling rather than direct MLP-based shadow regression.

Strengths  
• Innovative shadow-based formulation: The paper’s key contribution is exploiting cast shadows as a geometric cue to recover depth and surface detail from just a single viewpoint and multiple lighting conditions.  
• Efficient ray-tracing integration: Incorporating a ray tracer into the neural field’s optimization loop permits accurate shadow visibility estimation at reasonable cost, which is technically appealing and well engineered.

Weaknesses  
• Unrealistic lighting requirements: The method depends on real directional light sources with precisely known directions and calibration. Such a setup is only practical in laboratory or dedicated light-stage environments, limiting real-world adoption.  
• Limited evaluation on synthetic scenes: All experiments are conducted on simple, uncluttered synthetic objects and backgrounds. This controlled setting conceals challenges that would surface with real-world clutter, varied materials, and complex occluders.  
• Geometry–shadow surface entanglement: When the shadow-casting surface and the object’s geometry interact, ambiguities arise, making shape estimation unstable without strong priors.  
• Reconstruction quality and robustness: Compared to multi-view reconstruction methods, the proposed approach trails in accuracy. Furthermore, performance degrades sharply when the number of distinct light directions is reduced.  
• Overly demanding problem setup: Without incorporating stronger geometric or photometric priors, the approach tackles an impractically unconstrained problem, which raises doubts about its scalability and reliability.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Evaluate with softer shadows: Assess the system’s performance using area lights or larger sources to produce penumbrae. Demonstrating tolerance to soft shadows would broaden practical applicability.  
2. Compare shadow generation strategies: Provide an empirical comparison between the ray-traced shadow generation and a direct MLP-based regression baseline, to quantify the benefits of the current design.  
3. Enhance Figure 4: Include nearest-neighbor or other baseline reconstructions alongside the proposed results to better contextualize the gains.  
4. Discuss real-world scenarios: Elaborate on application domains (e.g., quality control in industrial inspection, heritage digitization with controlled lighting) where the proposed lighting calibration and setup could be justified.

Detailed Comments  
• Technical setup: The reliance on precise light calibration—potentially more stringent than typical camera pose calibration—may be a barrier for non-expert users. Some discussion of calibration tolerance or automated calibration strategies would strengthen the work.  
• Shadow computation: The root-finding and occupancy sampling scheme is well motivated, but it would be helpful to know its sensitivity to initialization and sampling density.  
• Light-count sensitivity: Since reconstruction quality plummets with fewer lights, an ablation studying the minimum number of lights needed for acceptable quality would clarify practical limits.

Conclusion  
This paper explores a novel direction in single-view reconstruction by leveraging cast shadows and an integrated ray-tracing module. While technically sound and promising in a controlled setting, the heavy reliance on calibrated directional lighting and the purely synthetic evaluation constrain its practical impact. Given the significant gap to multi-view methods in terms of robustness and accuracy, and the challenges in real-world deployment, I recommend a “4: Borderline Reject.” The authors may consider addressing the suggestions above to enhance applicability and broaden the evaluation in a revision.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper is about using neural radiance field to reconstruction the geometry of an object lit by varying directional light sources, leveraging cues of cast shadow. The optimization uses an efficient way to trace rays for shadow computation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The setup is interesting as in using shadow cues to reconstruct 3D geometry when multi-view captures are not available. The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This highly controlled lighting setup seems to only be feasible in a laboratory or a light stage setting. This may be the reason the paper only shows toy synthetic examples. Another 'contrived' setup is in the simple scene / background. Cast shadow depends on the object geometry, the lighting, and also the surface the shadow is cast on. The geometry of the surface and the geometry of the object can be entangled to cause ambiguity. This is more severe in real-world examples. The task can be unnecessarily (due to such setup being unlikely in real-world scenarios) challenging and ambiguous without any priors on the object geometry. The reconstructed geometry is not on par with multi-view settings, and degrades drastically as the number of lights decreases. My biggest concern about this paper is it makes the reconstruction task unnecessarily challenging and impractical.

Questions:
- to make the setup slightly more practical, how well does the method perform when shadow is softer, i.e., slightly larger light source, or small area lights?

- line 149: has the proposed shadow generation method been validated over direct regression method using an MLP?

- fig 4: should also show the nearest neighbor training samples

Limitations:
The calibrated lighting is more demanding than calibrated camera pose, which can be easily acquired, even though not entirely accurate, on most modern smartphones. The lighting needs to be real directional light source, which is difficult to acquire in real-world scenarios.

It would be also valuable to discuss potential applications for the proposed setup and algorithm.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a promising framework for near-field photometric stereo utilizing volume and surface rendering. The proposed method is tested on a synthesis dataset and a real dataset. Their results have reached state-of-the-art.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
---Originality---
1) The overall idea shows the originality, especially in how to combine surface and volume rendering.
2) Imposing the shadow's constraint on the occupancy field in the 3D space is enlighting. 
---Quality---
1) Eq.8: the author used $C_v$ to denote the volume-rendered image and $C_s$ to indicate surface rendered image. However, no formula illustrates how $C_v$ is calculated, which  confuses reading. 
2) Figure 2: two "MLP" blocks are shown in two separate branches, which makes me think there are two different MLPs estimating occupancy along the surface-to-light segment and the camera ray. However, according to Eq.6, the MLP for shadow handling is identical to the MLP for the occupancy field. Please try to redraw this part.
---Clarity---
1) Figure 2's caption, lines 2-3: How the points along the rays are sampled is unclear to me. The author mentions to sample $N_V$ points around the surface and $N_L$ points on the surface-to-light segment. I assume $N_L$ points are sampled uniformly, but without knowing the scene's scale, it is unclear how to ensure the sampled points along the camera ray are "around the surface."
2) Although the author tries to explain the necessity of the shadow clues for surface reconstruction in Section 4.4 and Section B in the supplementary material, it is still unclear to me why the shadow becomes a strong constraint on the model that can substitute the constraint from multi-view photo-consistency. There are two kinds of cast shadows in the synthesis dataset, including the foreground shadow on the object, and the background shadow. According to the results in section F, table S2, and section 4.2 table 2, the visibility of the background shadow is very important for depth estimation. The author should analyze the contribution of two different kinds of shadows separately.
3) According to the presenting results, the proposed method has reached an ideal visual and quantitative result in their synthetic dataset. Even the background is well reconstructed with little artifacts. However, the author didn't directly explain why their method is not sensitive to the background (it is almost perfect) and can reconstruct an accurate background simultaneously. (e.g., UNISURF [A] applies NeRF++ [B] for the complex background and assumes black for the simple background. However, their reconstructed background still contains visible artifacts).

[A] Michael Oechsle, Songyou Peng, and Andreas Geiger. UNISURF: Unifying neural implicit surfaces and radiance fields for multi-view reconstruction. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV). 2021

[B] Kai Zhang, Gernot Riegler, Noah Snavely, and Vladlen Koltun. Nerf++: Analyzing and improving neural radiance fields. arXiv.org, 2020.

---Significance---

1) There is no intuitive explanation for the role of modeling shadow for shape estimation. What is the problem aiming to solve by considering the shadow, the ambiguity, additional clue of global shape information, useful clue for depth estimation, or even light refinement? 
2) The evaluation is mainly conducted on the synthetic dataset built by the authors, which is less convincing. Although the paper shows the result of the LUCES dataset in the supplementary material, the depth estimation is not satisfactory (4.39 vs. 3.17 for the state-of-the-art). This evaluation could not provide strong support to the proposed method.
3) The compared methods, NeRF and UNISURF, are implemented in a naive way. It is unclear whether the authors have also sampled points along the light ray in those methods. If not, it can be expected that the reconstruction results are not good because NeRF and UNISURF are mainly applied in multi-view stereo, which is constrained by the multi-view photo consistency that is lacking in single-view photometric stereo. Therefore, using them for comparison is less convincing. 
4) As said before, the background shadow's visibility greatly influences the performance. Therefore, this work has lots of pre-setting to the environment (the background is better to be the light color, and the intensity should be bright enough), which will limit the application scope of this method. Moreover, The background's complexity may influence the background shadow's visibility and the shading of the scenes, which further impact the performance of the methods. While the background in the synthesis dataset and LUCES is still an ideal case, there is a lack of such discussions on a more realistic scenario.
reviews.questions: Except for the problems listed above, I wonder how well the proposed method can perform under a more complicated background.
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed parts of the limitations and potential negative social  impact of their work. However, the impacts of the background's complexity and the shadow's visibility are not stated.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a promising framework for near-field photometric stereo utilizing volume and surface rendering. The proposed method is tested on a synthesis dataset and a real dataset. Their results have reached state-of-the-art.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
---Originality---
1) The overall idea shows the originality, especially in how to combine surface and volume rendering.
2) Imposing the shadow's constraint on the occupancy field in the 3D space is enlighting. 
---Quality---
1) Eq.8: the author used $C_v$ to denote the volume-rendered image and $C_s$ to indicate surface rendered image. However, no formula illustrates how $C_v$ is calculated, which  confuses reading. 
2) Figure 2: two "MLP" blocks are shown in two separate branches, which makes me think there are two different MLPs estimating occupancy along the surface-to-light segment and the camera ray. However, according to Eq.6, the MLP for shadow handling is identical to the MLP for the occupancy field. Please try to redraw this part.
---Clarity---
1) Figure 2's caption, lines 2-3: How the points along the rays are sampled is unclear to me. The author mentions to sample $N_V$ points around the surface and $N_L$ points on the surface-to-light segment. I assume $N_L$ points are sampled uniformly, but without knowing the scene's scale, it is unclear how to ensure the sampled points along the camera ray are "around the surface."
2) Although the author tries to explain the necessity of the shadow clues for surface reconstruction in Section 4.4 and Section B in the supplementary material, it is still unclear to me why the shadow becomes a strong constraint on the model that can substitute the constraint from multi-view photo-consistency. There are two kinds of cast shadows in the synthesis dataset, including the foreground shadow on the object, and the background shadow. According to the results in section F, table S2, and section 4.2 table 2, the visibility of the background shadow is very important for depth estimation. The author should analyze the contribution of two different kinds of shadows separately.
3) According to the presenting results, the proposed method has reached an ideal visual and quantitative result in their synthetic dataset. Even the background is well reconstructed with little artifacts. However, the author didn't directly explain why their method is not sensitive to the background (it is almost perfect) and can reconstruct an accurate background simultaneously. (e.g., UNISURF [A] applies NeRF++ [B] for the complex background and assumes black for the simple background. However, their reconstructed background still contains visible artifacts).

[A] Michael Oechsle, Songyou Peng, and Andreas Geiger. UNISURF: Unifying neural implicit surfaces and radiance fields for multi-view reconstruction. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV). 2021

[B] Kai Zhang, Gernot Riegler, Noah Snavely, and Vladlen Koltun. Nerf++: Analyzing and improving neural radiance fields. arXiv.org, 2020.

---Significance---

1) There is no intuitive explanation for the role of modeling shadow for shape estimation. What is the problem aiming to solve by considering the shadow, the ambiguity, additional clue of global shape information, useful clue for depth estimation, or even light refinement? 
2) The evaluation is mainly conducted on the synthetic dataset built by the authors, which is less convincing. Although the paper shows the result of the LUCES dataset in the supplementary material, the depth estimation is not satisfactory (4.39 vs. 3.17 for the state-of-the-art). This evaluation could not provide strong support to the proposed method.
3) The compared methods, NeRF and UNISURF, are implemented in a naive way. It is unclear whether the authors have also sampled points along the light ray in those methods. If not, it can be expected that the reconstruction results are not good because NeRF and UNISURF are mainly applied in multi-view stereo, which is constrained by the multi-view photo consistency that is lacking in single-view photometric stereo. Therefore, using them for comparison is less convincing. 
4) As said before, the background shadow's visibility greatly influences the performance. Therefore, this work has lots of pre-setting to the environment (the background is better to be the light color, and the intensity should be bright enough), which will limit the application scope of this method. Moreover, The background's complexity may influence the background shadow's visibility and the shading of the scenes, which further impact the performance of the methods. While the background in the synthesis dataset and LUCES is still an ideal case, there is a lack of such discussions on a more realistic scenario.

Questions:
Except for the problems listed above, I wonder how well the proposed method can perform under a more complicated background.

Limitations:
The authors have addressed parts of the limitations and potential negative social  impact of their work. However, the impacts of the background's complexity and the shadow's visibility are not stated.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Response:

title: Robust Binary Models by Pruning Randomly-initialized Networks

reviews.summary: This paper presents a method for searching binary randomly initialized networks to generate pruned and robust models. The paper leverages the existing edge-popup algorithm for pruning untrained networks via learning important scores instead of model weights. The paper also proposes adding a last layer batchnorm to stabilize the training of their compressed robust models. The paper conducts several experiments to support their claim of achieving state-of-the-art robust binary networks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The paper is well motivated
- The quality of writing is good, the ideas are well explained
- The experimental results support the authors' claims, achieving state-of-the-art results for pruned binary robust models

Weaknesses:
- The novelty of this work is a bit limited, as it combines existing techniques (edge-popup + adversarial loss similar to [1] and [2]), that being said the end results are very impressive
- I would have liked to see more analysis on why the method is so successful, as opposed to adversarial training and/or binarization/pruning schemes


[1] Ramanujan, V., Wortsman, M., Kembhavi, A., Farhadi, A., & Rastegari, M. (2020). What's hidden in a randomly weighted neural network?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (pp. 11893-11902).

[2] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.
reviews.questions:
1) In the Signed Kaiming Constant initialization (SKC) in line 175, is the 1-r a typo? since the original paper does not have the pruning ratio in the initialization step. If it is not a typo, does that mean every pruning ratio requires a different randomly initialized network?

2) Is the last normalization layer a standard batchnorm layer, or a homogenous (affine=False) batchnorm layer as used in other layers?

3) The observation that more pruning leads to higher robustness is very intriguing, especially when existing pruning + adversarial training methods, such as HYDRA [1] and ADMM [2], typically show that robustness decreases with the pruning rate (as is expected). Moreover, the edge-pop up algorithm typically achieves its best performance (in the non adversarial setting) when r=0.5, as it maximizes the number of possible network choices. Could the authors shed more light on the reason why their method seems to contradict both these existing observations?

4) If I understood correctly, the proposed method is able to generate 99% pruned binary networks, that achieve adversarial robustness that is higher than all other baselines, which includes full precision adversarially trained models and their pruned versions, as well as other trained binary models. For context, the state-of-the-art 99% pruned full precision baseline achieved by HYDRA suffers massive degradation in terms of robustness, when compared to an unpruned baseline. The results reported in this paper are very impressive and also very intriguing, as they seems too good to be true. I believe more experiments/analysis is warranted to explain why this method is so much more effective than prior work. One simple experiment perhaps is to initialize the weights to constant values between [-1,1] instead of the binary {-1,1}, and keep everything else the same. One would expect that pruning this baseline should have even better robust accuracy than the binarized version, and thus surpass that of any FP baseline. Another question is what would happen if we train the pruned models from scratch (that is take the pruning mask and initialize another model for adversarial training). It would be great if the authors can address this. 

[1] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

[2] Ye, S., Xu, K., Liu, S., Cheng, H., Lambrechts, J. H., Zhang, H., ... & Lin, X. (2019). Adversarial robustness vs. model compression, or both?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (pp. 111-120).

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a method for searching binary randomly initialized networks to generate pruned and robust models. The paper leverages the existing edge-popup algorithm for pruning untrained networks via learning important scores instead of model weights. The paper also proposes adding a last layer batchnorm to stabilize the training of their compressed robust models. The paper conducts several experiments to support their claim of achieving state-of-the-art robust binary networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The paper is well motivated
- The quality of writing is good, the ideas are well explained
- The experimental results support the authors' claims, achieving state-of-the-art results for pruned binary robust models

Weaknesses:
- The novelty of this work is a bit limited, as it combines existing techniques (edge-popup + adversarial loss similar to [1] and [2]), that being said the end results are very impressive
- I would have liked to see more analysis on why the method is so successful, as opposed to adversarial training and/or binarization/pruning schemes


[1] Ramanujan, V., Wortsman, M., Kembhavi, A., Farhadi, A., & Rastegari, M. (2020). What's hidden in a randomly weighted neural network?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (pp. 11893-11902).

[2] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

Questions:
1) In the Signed Kaiming Constant initialization (SKC) in line 175, is the 1-r a typo? since the original paper does not have the pruning ratio in the initialization step. If it is not a typo, does that mean every pruning ratio requires a different randomly initialized network?

2) Is the last normalization layer a standard batchnorm layer, or a homogenous (affine=False) batchnorm layer as used in other layers?

3) The observation that more pruning leads to higher robustness is very intriguing, especially when existing pruning + adversarial training methods, such as HYDRA [1] and ADMM [2], typically show that robustness decreases with the pruning rate (as is expected). Moreover, the edge-pop up algorithm typically achieves its best performance (in the non adversarial setting) when r=0.5, as it maximizes the number of possible network choices. Could the authors shed more light on the reason why their method seems to contradict both these existing observations?

4) If I understood correctly, the proposed method is able to generate 99% pruned binary networks, that achieve adversarial robustness that is higher than all other baselines, which includes full precision adversarially trained models and their pruned versions, as well as other trained binary models. For context, the state-of-the-art 99% pruned full precision baseline achieved by HYDRA suffers massive degradation in terms of robustness, when compared to an unpruned baseline. The results reported in this paper are very impressive and also very intriguing, as they seems too good to be true. I believe more experiments/analysis is warranted to explain why this method is so much more effective than prior work. One simple experiment perhaps is to initialize the weights to constant values between [-1,1] instead of the binary {-1,1}, and keep everything else the same. One would expect that pruning this baseline should have even better robust accuracy than the binarized version, and thus surpass that of any FP baseline. Another question is what would happen if we train the pruned models from scratch (that is take the pruning mask and initialize another model for adversarial training). It would be great if the authors can address this. 

[1] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

[2] Ye, S., Xu, K., Liu, S., Cheng, H., Lambrechts, J. H., Zhang, H., ... & Lin, X. (2019). Adversarial robustness vs. model compression, or both?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (pp. 111-120).

Limitations:
The authors claim that their work is generic and does not suffer from limitations or potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper investigates the training of highly pruned, binary neural networks for adversarial robustness. Building on the edge-popup pruning algorithm applied to randomly initialized binary weights, the authors introduce a final batch‐normalization layer to stabilize training. Empirical results on standard vision benchmarks demonstrate that their method achieves state‐of‐the‐art robust accuracy—even at pruning ratios up to 99%—and in fact shows improved robustness as the pruning rate increases. While the experiments are thorough and the presentation is clear, the novelty is mainly in the recombination of existing techniques (edge-popup, adversarial loss, batchnorm), and the surprising trend of enhanced robustness under extreme sparsity lacks a deeper explanation.

Strengths  
1. Motivation and Clarity  
   • The paper addresses an important problem at the intersection of model compression and adversarial robustness.  
   • The writing is concise and well organized, making the methodological choices easy to follow.  

2. Empirical Rigor  
   • Extensive experiments validate the claims, consistently showing that binary pruned subnetworks found via edge-popup plus a final batchnorm outperform full‐precision baselines under strong attacks.  
   • The demonstrated robustness at very high pruning rates (up to 99%) is impressive and, to our knowledge, unprecedented in prior pruning or binarization literature.  

Weaknesses  
1. Incremental Novelty  
   • The primary components—edge-popup for pruning, binary weight initialization, adversarial training, and batch normalization—are all established techniques. The paper’s contribution lies largely in combining these ingredients rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms.  

2. Lack of Mechanistic Insight  
   • The most striking empirical result is that higher sparsity yields greater robustness, which contradicts earlier findings from HYDRA and ADMM‐based pruning schemes. However, the paper offers only speculative discussion rather than any analytical or empirical investigation into why this occurs.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Initialization Clarification  
   • The Signed Kaiming Constant initialization in the manuscript includes a (1−r) scaling factor dependent on the pruning ratio r. Please confirm whether this term is a typographical error or an intentional modification, and justify its role if it is required.  

2. Details on Final BatchNorm  
   • Specify whether the last batch‐normalization layer is implemented with affine=False (no learnable scale and shift) or the standard affine batchnorm. This choice can materially affect performance and should be precisely stated.  

3. Understanding the Robustness–Pruning Trend  
   • Provide additional analysis to explain why extreme pruning improves adversarial robustness. For example, can you characterize how the gradient landscape or margin distribution changes as sparsity increases?  

4. Weight Initialization Ablation  
   • As a control, consider initializing the network weights to constant values in [−1,1] (rather than binary ±1) before running edge-popup. Comparing the robustness of these differently initialized subnetworks would shed light on the importance of binary initialization.  

5. Retraining Subnetworks with Adversarial Training  
   • An ablation in which you fix the discovered mask and re‐train the subnetwork from scratch under adversarial training (without edge-popup) could reveal whether the mask itself encodes robustness or if joint mask discovery is crucial.  

Overall Assessment and Recommendation  
This work is technically solid, experimentally thorough, and addresses a timely topic in robust model compression. Although the novelty is largely incremental, the empirical finding that extreme sparsity can enhance adversarial robustness is both surprising and potentially high‐impact for practitioners. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 7).

Summary:
The paper proposes a method for learning robust binary neural networks from random initialization. The proposed training scheme includes  adaptive pruning approach and an edge pop-up algorithm for training the binary model to obtain a binary mask for pruning using a PGD-like method. It shows clear improvement over other adversarial training methods for binary models in experiments. Some theoretical analysis for the adaptive pruning method is provided.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths
- The training algorithm is clearly stated makes sense. 
- The experimental results are impressive with evident improvement over comparing methods.

- The ablation study and sensitivity analysis are very clear and are consistent with intuition. For example, the ablation study for the hyper-parameter $p$ for adaptive pruning aligns very well with the analysis.

### Weakness

Questions:
- Can the authors provide some intuition or analysis regarding why this training scheme could work?

Limitations:

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a novel approach for training robust binary neural networks directly from random initialization. The core contributions are an adaptive pruning strategy that automatically determines per-layer pruning rates, coupled with an “edge pop-up” algorithm which learns a binary mask via a PGD-style update. The authors complement these algorithmic innovations with theoretical analysis of their adaptive pruning hyperparameter p, and provide an extensive experimental evaluation, including ablation and sensitivity studies. Overall, the method yields significant robustness gains over existing adversarial training techniques for binary models.

Strengths  
1. Clear and well-motivated training algorithm. The manuscript lays out both the adaptive pruning procedure and the edge pop-up mask learning in a transparent, step-by-step fashion. 2. Strong empirical performance. Experiments on ResNet34 demonstrate consistent, sizable improvements in adversarial robustness compared to prior binary model defenses. 3. Theoretical support. The authors offer a concise theoretical analysis explaining how the adaptive pruning hyperparameter p influences mask sparsity across layers, lending insight into the design choices. 4. Thorough ablation and sensitivity analysis. The impact of key hyperparameters—particularly p—is explored in depth, and the results match the theoretical predictions, which strengthens confidence in the method.

Weakness  
• Limited model diversity. All experiments are restricted to a single architecture (ResNet34). This leaves open the question of how broadly applicable the proposed training scheme is across other network families.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Intuitive explanation. While the technical description is clear, adding more intuition or a high-level discussion about why adaptive pruning plus edge pop-up synergize to improve robustness would help readers internalize the method’s underlying principles. 2. Broader model evaluation. To demonstrate generality, it would be valuable in future work to apply the same binary training pipeline to other architectures—e.g., binary Transformers or other popular CNN backbones—and report robustness results.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
I find this work to be technically sound, well-presented, and accompanied by solid theoretical and empirical evidence. The proposed adaptive pruning and edge pop-up scheme represents a meaningful advance in training adversarially robust binary networks. While exploring additional architectures would further strengthen the paper, the current contributions merit acceptance. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 7).

reviews.summary: This manuscript extends the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to binary networks and proposed a new method to obtain compact binary networks with better robustness. The proposed method consists of three main parts: a) adaptively pruning different network layers; b) an effective binary initialization scheme; c) adding the last batch normalization layer. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100 validate the effectiveness of the method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
#### Strength:

1. The paper is well written with sufficient details of the proposed methods.
2. Experimental results on three datasets show superior performance against multiple baseline methods, including vanilla AT, Flying Bird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC.
3. Analysis of the derived sparse patterns is provided.

#### Weakness:

1. The proposed methods search for sparse binary subnetworks within randomly-initialized networks. It's unclear whether such a scheme is better than training the parameters directly. It seems the proposed methods (adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and last batch normalization layer) can also to used to optimize the parameters of randomly-initialized networks. 
2. As the proposed methods aim to obtain binary neural networks, which are both compact and robust. The comparison about training and inference cost (e.g., FLOPs) is important.
3. It seems better to firstly report the comparison results in Section 4.2, and then conduct the ablation study of three proposed methods (Section 4.1).
reviews.limitations: To some extent, this paper may help to build more secure and compact deep learning systems in the real world. But the real-world system faces all types of attacks, which are far beyond adversarial examples.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This manuscript extends the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to binary networks and proposed a new method to obtain compact binary networks with better robustness. The proposed method consists of three main parts: a) adaptively pruning different network layers; b) an effective binary initialization scheme; c) adding the last batch normalization layer. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100 validate the effectiveness of the method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
#### Strength:

1. The paper is well written with sufficient details of the proposed methods.
2. Experimental results on three datasets show superior performance against multiple baseline methods, including vanilla AT, Flying Bird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC.
3. Analysis of the derived sparse patterns is provided.

#### Weakness:

1. The proposed methods search for sparse binary subnetworks within randomly-initialized networks. It's unclear whether such a scheme is better than training the parameters directly. It seems the proposed methods (adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and last batch normalization layer) can also to used to optimize the parameters of randomly-initialized networks. 
2. As the proposed methods aim to obtain binary neural networks, which are both compact and robust. The comparison about training and inference cost (e.g., FLOPs) is important.
3. It seems better to firstly report the comparison results in Section 4.2, and then conduct the ablation study of three proposed methods (Section 4.1).

Limitations:
To some extent, this paper may help to build more secure and compact deep learning systems in the real world. But the real-world system faces all types of attacks, which are far beyond adversarial examples.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
This paper presents a compelling extension of the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to the realm of robust binary networks. By integrating three key components—adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary weight initialization scheme, and an additional final batch‐normalization layer—the authors obtain sparse binary subnetworks that achieve strong robust accuracy on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-100. Empirically, their method outperforms several contemporary baselines, including vanilla adversarial training (AT), FlyingBird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC. Furthermore, the paper offers an insightful analysis of the emergent sparsity patterns in the resulting subnetworks.

Strengths  
1. The methodology is articulated clearly and in good detail, making it straightforward to follow the pruning schedule, initialization procedure, and final layer normalization.  
2. Experimental results on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100 are strong: the proposed binary subnetworks consistently surpass multiple prior methods in robust accuracy under adversarial attacks.  
3. The authors supplement their performance claims with an analysis of the derived sparse patterns, which deepens our understanding of how robust binary structures differ from their dense or real-valued counterparts.

Weaknesses  
1. It remains unclear whether the process of searching for a sparse binary subnetwork from a randomly initialized model offers a substantive advantage over simply training the same binary network parameters directly via standard optimization.  
2. No quantitative comparison of training or inference cost is provided. Claims of efficiency would be far more convincing if supported by FLOPs counts, runtime measurements, or similar metrics.  
3. The organization of the manuscript could be improved. In particular, the main comparison against baselines (currently in Section 4.2) appears after the ablation study, which may confuse readers trying to grasp the overall gains before delving into component analyses.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Conduct a direct experimental comparison between (a) searching for sparse binary subnetworks and (b) training binary networks end-to-end on the same architectures. This will clarify whether the lottery-ticket approach truly outperforms parameter optimization from scratch.  
• Include detailed metrics for both training and inference cost—e.g., total FLOPs, wall-clock runtime on a fixed hardware setup, or both. These will strengthen claims about the method’s efficiency.  
• Reorder the manuscript so that benchmarking against existing methods is presented before the ablation studies. This will improve the logical flow and help readers first appreciate the overall performance gains and then understand which components drive them.

Overall, this work is technically sound and presents an important step toward understanding robust binary networks through the lens of the lottery ticket hypothesis. While I believe the paper would benefit from additional cost analysis and minor restructuring, the contributions and empirical strengths outweigh these limitations. I therefore recommend a borderline accept.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept

Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22].
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths.
1) In Section 1 and Section 3.1 (Equation (2)), the paper clearly introduces the base model [19] that the paper works on: a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis.
2) In Section 3.2 and Section 3.2, introduction of the proposed three techniques is easy-to-follow, except too more "In Appendix ...". The proposed three techniques, adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer, are reasonable.
3) Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22]. The paper further provides necessary analysis on the subnetwork patterns, and sensitivity analysis on the pruning strategy and added last batch-normalization layer.

Weaknesses.
1) My major concern is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies. For example, the proposed adaptive pruning is a straightforward trade-off between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters, and the key parameter $p$ is manually but not automatically selected in all experiments. Moreover, the paper works on a base model [19] which is already a robust binary model. The paper focuses on model compression only but not further improving binarization and adversarial robustness. 
2) In Table 4, different methods use different $p$, e.g., $p=1.0$ for RST and $p=0.1$ for the proposed method. I think all methods should use the same $p$ value for fair comparison.
3) Since tiny-scale datasets CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are easily to be overfitting. I suggest the authors use large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet for experiments.

Summary:
For a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, the paper proposes a model compress method with three strategies: adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22].

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths.
1) In Section 1 and Section 3.1 (Equation (2)), the paper clearly introduces the base model [19] that the paper works on: a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis.
2) In Section 3.2 and Section 3.2, introduction of the proposed three techniques is easy-to-follow, except too more "In Appendix ...". The proposed three techniques, adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer, are reasonable.
3) Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22]. The paper further provides necessary analysis on the subnetwork patterns, and sensitivity analysis on the pruning strategy and added last batch-normalization layer.

Weaknesses.
1) My major concern is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies. For example, the proposed adaptive pruning is a straightforward trade-off between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters, and the key parameter $p$ is manually but not automatically selected in all experiments. Moreover, the paper works on a base model [19] which is already a robust binary model. The paper focuses on model compression only but not further improving binarization and adversarial robustness. 
2) In Table 4, different methods use different $p$, e.g., $p=1.0$ for RST and $p=0.1$ for the proposed method. I think all methods should use the same $p$ value for fair comparison.
3) Since tiny-scale datasets CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are easily to be overfitting. I suggest the authors use large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet for experiments.

Questions:
1) Are the proposed three techniques useful for regular DNNs in addition to the robust binary models [19]?
2) Why do the methods in Table 4 use different $p$ value? Are the methods in Table 4 fairly compared with each other?
3) Is the proposed method scalable to large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet?

Limitations:
As mentioned above, a major limitation is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies.

4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary  
This paper addresses the challenge of improving adversarial robustness in binary neural networks by building on a recently proposed robust binary base model and adding three auxiliary techniques: adaptive layer‐wise pruning, a specialized binary initialization, and a last‐batch normalization step. The authors clearly formulate the underlying robust binary model (Sections 1 and 3.1) and then describe their three add‐on strategies in Section 3.2. Empirically, they demonstrate impressive gains on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100: their approach outperforms several prior binarization and robust training methods, and they provide insightful analyses of subnetwork structure and sensitivity to pruning.

Strengths  
1. The base robust binary model and its adversarial training formulation are introduced with clarity, making it easy to follow the paper’s starting point.  
2. Each of the three proposed techniques—adaptive layer‐wise pruning, binary initialization, and last‐batch normalization—is described in detail, with intuitive motivation and pseudocode.  
3. Experimental results on CIFAR-10/100 are strong: the method consistently beats prior robust binarization approaches, and the authors back up their claims with thorough subnetwork pattern and ablation studies.

Weaknesses  
1. The three techniques feel like auxiliary model‐compression add-ons rather than components of a unified framework. There is no overarching principle that ties pruning, initialization, and normalization into a systematic methodology.  
2. The adaptive layer‐wise pruning relies on a trade-off parameter p that the authors manually choose. This ad hoc tuning undermines claims of automation and robustness—especially since p interpolates between a fixed pruning rate and a fixed parameter count.  
3. The paper’s novelty is limited by its reliance on an existing robust binary base model. The main contributions are incremental improvements on binarization and adversarial robustness rather than fundamental advances.  
4. Fairness concerns arise in the comparison of methods: in Table 4, RST is evaluated with p = 1.0 while the proposed scheme uses p = 0.1. This inconsistent hyperparameter setting may bias the results.  
5. All experiments are conducted on relatively small benchmarks (CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100). This narrow evaluation raises questions about the method’s scalability and generality to larger, more challenging datasets.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Develop a more systematic, unifying framework that integrates pruning, initialization, and normalization under a common theoretical or algorithmic umbrella.  
2. Automate the selection of the adaptive‐pruning hyperparameter p, for instance via data‐driven criteria or a validation‐based meta‐optimization, to reduce manual tuning.  
3. Ensure a fair comparison by fixing p (or other key hyperparameters) across all methods in Table 4, so that each approach is evaluated under the same conditions.  
4. Extend the experimental evaluation to larger and more diverse datasets (e.g., TinyImageNet or ImageNet subsets) to validate that the observed empirical gains scale beyond CIFAR-10/100.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This paper offers a clear presentation and achieves noteworthy empirical improvements on CIFAR datasets. However, the contributions currently amount to auxiliary enhancements to an existing robust binary model, with manual hyperparameter tuning and potential fairness issues in the comparisons. Combined with the limited evaluation scope, these concerns temper the overall impact. I therefore lean toward a borderline rejection (Rating 4), as the identified weaknesses outweigh the contributions in their present form.

reviews.text:
Review for “Event-based, Activity-Sparse GRU (EGRU)”

Summary  
This paper presents EGRU, a novel variant of the gated recurrent unit that processes input events in an activity-sparse fashion. By updating only a subset of hidden units when new events arrive, EGRU reduces the number of multiply–accumulate (MAC) operations during both inference and training. The authors develop a clear theoretical framework to characterize the sparsity induced by the event-based updates, and they validate their claims with experiments on multiple sequence modeling tasks. The manuscript is well written, situates itself thoroughly within the related-work landscape, and provides extensive empirical evidence of competitive accuracy alongside significant reductions in computational cost and energy consumption.

Strengths  
1. Novelty and Technical Soundness: Introducing event-driven, activity-sparse computation into a GRU architecture is an original contribution that advances the state of the art in sparse recurrent models. The theoretical derivations supporting the sparsity claims are rigorous and correctly applied.  
2. Comprehensive Evaluation: The authors conduct experiments on several benchmarks, reporting not only task accuracy but also the number of MAC operations and measured sparsity levels. These metrics convincingly demonstrate that EGRU achieves similar predictive performance to dense GRUs while using far fewer compute resources.  
3. Related Work Coverage: The paper provides an in-depth review of prior approaches to sparsity and event-based processing in RNNs, clearly distinguishing EGRU from existing methods.  
4. Clarity and Organization: The writing is crisp, figures are clear, and the overall flow— from motivation through theory to experimental results— is easy to follow.  
5. Practical Impact: By cutting down on computation during both inference and training, EGRU shows significant promise for deployment in energy-constrained settings such as edge devices or event-driven sensors.

Weaknesses  
1. Missing Comparison to Pruning Methods: While EGRU’s activity sparsity is well quantified, the paper does not directly compare its sparsity–efficiency trade-off against GRUs made sparse via pruning techniques (e.g., as in “Exploring Sparsity in Recurrent Neural Networks”). Such a comparison would clarify where event-based sparsity stands relative to structured or unstructured weight sparsity.  
2. Inherited Societal Risks: As a variant of GRUs, EGRU inherits the same potential for biases or misuse found in standard recurrent architectures. This limitation is acknowledged but not explored in depth.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Include an empirical comparison between activity-sparse EGRU and pruning-based sparse GRUs. This could involve matching overall sparsity levels or computational budgets to illustrate the relative merits of event-based versus weight-pruning approaches.  
– Briefly discuss or outline mitigation strategies for standard RNN societal risks (e.g., dataset bias, adversarial vulnerabilities), even if only to confirm that EGRU does not exacerbate these issues.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper offers a well-motivated and technically solid approach to bringing event-driven sparsity into recurrent models. The theoretical analysis is sound, and the experimental evidence supports claims of competitive accuracy with reduced computation and energy requirements. The main omission— a direct comparison to pruning methods— is easily addressed and does not detract from the core contribution. I therefore recommend Weak Accept (6), as the work has moderate-to-high impact, is reproducible, and poses no major ethical or evaluation concerns.

reviews.text:
Review for “Event-Based Continuous-Time GRU (EGRU) with Event-Based Backpropagation”

Summary  
This paper introduces the Event-based GRU (EGRU), a novel continuous-time recurrent unit that processes input events asynchronously and employs an event-based backpropagation algorithm. The authors demonstrate that EGRU achieves competitive accuracy on several sequence benchmarks while maintaining high activity-based sparsity in both hidden state updates and gradient computations.

Strengths  
1. Novelty: The proposed EGRU architecture and its event-driven training scheme represent a fresh contribution to sparse recurrent modeling.  
2. Clarity: The manuscript is well written, with intuitive figures and clear descriptions of both the forward dynamics and the backpropagation through events.  
3. Empirical Results: Initial experiments indicate that EGRU matches or outperforms baselines on multiple tasks, achieving impressive sparsity levels without sacrificing accuracy.

Weaknesses  
1. Lack of Ablation Study: The paper does not isolate the effects of individual design components (e.g., gating mechanisms, event thresholds, continuous-time update rules).  
2. Insufficient Experimental Detail: Key hyper-parameter optimization procedures and the rationale behind learning-rate schedules, regularization settings, and event-threshold choices are not described.  
3. Missing Citations and Comparisons: Important related work on sparse recurrent networks (e.g., sparse RTRL methods and other event- or activity-driven architectures) is omitted, and no direct empirical comparison against at least two of these methods is provided.  
4. No Statistical Analysis: Results are reported for a single run per benchmark, with no indication of variance or statistical significance.  
5. Unquantified Computational Efficiency: Claims of reduced computation are not supported by FLOPS counts or detailed runtime measurements.  
6. Robustness and Stability: There is no discussion of sensitivity to random seed, initialization, or alternative training conditions.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Ablation Study: Include controlled experiments that individually disable or vary each core component of EGRU to quantify its contribution to final performance and sparsity.  
2. Experimental Details: Provide a comprehensive account of hyper-parameter search methodology, optimization protocols, and the reasoning behind chosen values for key parameters.  
3. Expanded Comparisons: Add citations to and empirical baselines from sparse RTRL and other sparse recurrent models (e.g., references [1–5]), ensuring at least two direct comparisons in the main tables.  
4. Multiple Trials and Statistics: Run each experiment for K independent trials, reporting mean performance and standard error to establish the statistical significance of observed gains.  
5. Robustness Analysis: Discuss how performance varies across random seeds and under different training regimes to demonstrate model stability.  
6. FLOPS-Based Efficiency: Calculate and report the number of floating-point operations required for both training and inference, comparing EGRU against dense GRUs and other sparse RNNs.

Recommendation: Weak Accept  
Overall, this work presents a technically sound and potentially impactful contribution to event-driven recurrent modeling. While the experimental evaluation would benefit from deeper analyses and additional details, none of the identified gaps appears fundamentally fatal. Subject to the authors’ addressing the above suggestions, I recommend acceptance under a “Weak Accept” rating.

* Statistical significance: - Experiments should be conducted for K trials and average performance and the standard error should be reported.
* Can authors comment on the robustness and stability of the given model? Based on different seeds, and settings, the optimizer is model stable compared to dense models trained using BPTT.

* Authors have mentioned that EGRU is computationally efficient, can authors provide numbers in FLOPS for both training and inference compared with other sparse and dense RNNs

[2] https://arxiv.org/abs/1602.03032
[3] https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.09208.pdf
[4] https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-021-05727-y
[5] https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/liu21p.html

Limitations:
Highlighted above

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes an event-based continuous-time variant of the GRU model, named EGRU, motivated by building scalable, energy-efficient deep recurrent models. The EGRU unit only outputs events when the internal states reach a trainable threshold, which makes it activity-sparsity naturally. Furthermore, the authors derive corresponding BP algorithms for updating EGRU and extend it to a discrete-time version that is easier to implement by popular machine learning architecture. As the experiments show, the EGRU model exhibits comparable performance to state-of-the-art recurrent network architectures for several tasks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This paper is well-written, consistent, and produces good results. By introducing the event generating mechanism, the EGRU model not only significantly reduces the multiply-accumulate operations but also preserves the performance that is comparable to the more computation-expensive GRU-based model. 

Strengths:
+ The event generating mechanism is a novel module inserted into the GRU model, which is proved to effectively reduce the computation of the original model.
+ The authors conduct three downstream applications to validate the effectiveness of the EGRU model, which are convincing and with comparable performances to previous methods.

Weakness:
- For the delay-copy task, the authors only show a simple example. How about increasing the number of EGRU units? Besides, did the events generating mechanism improve the memory ability, compared to the GRU model?
- In gesture prediction, the authors down sample frames into 32x32, which may affect the performance. I wonder how the input size affects the prediction accuracy with EGRU model. In general, the larger-resolution images contain more detailed information, which could be helpful for classification. 
- For language modeling experiments, the performance decreases with the increment of EGRU units. Can the authors give some explanation or the trend of performance related to the number of EGRU units?
reviews.questions:
This paper presents a novel GRU architecture, which balances the performance and computational complexity. 
- However, in the downstream application with EGRU, some experiments’ setups are unclear, as listed in Weakness. I expect the authors to clarify these points. 
- Besides, the authors apply EGRU in some special cases. Since the LSTM/GRU are applied widely in CV/NLP applications, can they be replaced by EGRU to reduce the computation complexity in general? 
- As GRU is simplified from LSTM, can event generating mechanism transfer to the LSTM model?
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in discussion section.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an event-based continuous-time variant of the GRU model, named EGRU, motivated by building scalable, energy-efficient deep recurrent models. The EGRU unit only outputs events when the internal states reach a trainable threshold, which makes it activity-sparsity naturally. Furthermore, the authors derive corresponding BP algorithms for updating EGRU and extend it to a discrete-time version that is easier to implement by popular machine learning architecture. As the experiments show, the EGRU model exhibits comparable performance to state-of-the-art recurrent network architectures for several tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
This paper is well-written, consistent, and produces good results. By introducing the event generating mechanism, the EGRU model not only significantly reduces the multiply-accumulate operations but also preserves the performance that is comparable to the more computation-expensive GRU-based model. 

Strengths:
+ The event generating mechanism is a novel module inserted into the GRU model, which is proved to effectively reduce the computation of the original model.
+ The authors conduct three downstream applications to validate the effectiveness of the EGRU model, which are convincing and with comparable performances to previous methods.

Weakness:
- For the delay-copy task, the authors only show a simple example. How about increasing the number of EGRU units? Besides, did the events generating mechanism improve the memory ability, compared to the GRU model?
- In gesture prediction, the authors down sample frames into 32x32, which may affect the performance. I wonder how the input size affects the prediction accuracy with EGRU model. In general, the larger-resolution images contain more detailed information, which could be helpful for classification. 
- For language modeling experiments, the performance decreases with the increment of EGRU units. Can the authors give some explanation or the trend of performance related to the number of EGRU units?

Questions:
This paper presents a novel GRU architecture, which balances the performance and computational complexity. 
- However, in the downstream application with EGRU, some experiments’ setups are unclear, as listed in Weakness. I expect the authors to clarify these points. 
- Besides, the authors apply EGRU in some special cases. Since the LSTM/GRU are applied widely in CV/NLP applications, can they be replaced by EGRU to reduce the computation complexity in general? 
- As GRU is simplified from LSTM, can event generating mechanism transfer to the LSTM model?

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in discussion section.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: DEQGAN: Learning the Loss Function for PINNs with Generative Adversarial Networks

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a method to solve ordinary and partial differential equations using GANs. Following in the tradition of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimised by replacing a hand-tuned loss function such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers, on several well-known differential equations.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1) The paper is very well-written, well-structured and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2) The ablation study, and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
2) The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations -- in particular, with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

Weaknesses:
1) Many of the details of training, hyperparameters etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper, and hinder readability.
2) Some of the results require a lot more elucidation: e.g. the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, and thus make the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text. 
3) The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator, but not much more).
4) The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilised training, neither of these are nescessarily novel contributions in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalisation, skip connections in the generator, or with different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.
reviews.questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)
reviews.limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
