Summary:
The paper proposes a method to solve ordinary and partial differential equations using GANs. Following in the tradition of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimised by replacing a hand-tuned loss function such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers, on several well-known differential equations.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1) The paper is very well-written, well-structured and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2) The ablation study, and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
2) The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations -- in particular, with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

Weaknesses:
1) Many of the details of training, hyperparameters etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper, and hinder readability.
2) Some of the results require a lot more elucidation: e.g. the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, and thus make the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text. 
3) The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator, but not much more).
4) The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilised training, neither of these are nescessarily novel contributions in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalisation, skip connections in the generator, or with different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.

Questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)

Limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces a novel generative adversarial network (GAN) framework for learning loss functions in physics‐informed neural networks (PINNs) applied to ordinary and partial differential equations. By replacing hand‐crafted PINN losses with a learned GAN‐based objective, the authors demonstrate superior empirical performance over classical PINNs (using fixed L₁, L₂, and Huber losses) and even conventional numerical solvers on a range of benchmark problems. To address the notorious instability of GAN training, they employ instance noise and a residual‐based early‐stopping criterion. An extensive experimental evaluation, including an ablation study, provides evidence that the proposed approach yields more consistent convergence and lower error in tasks such as the modified Einstein equations.

Strengths  
1. The manuscript is very well written and logically organized. The methodology and experiments are described clearly, making it easy to follow the core idea of replacing manually tuned physics losses with a learned GAN discriminator.  
2. The ablation study on stabilization techniques (instance noise and residual monitoring) is thoughtful and highly relevant, given the challenges of GAN training in scientific applications.  
3. The experimental section is comprehensive. Through multiple repeated runs, the authors convincingly illustrate the high variance of classical PINNs and the consistent advantage of their GAN‐based loss over both PINNs and standard numerical methods.

Weaknesses  
1. Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4 devote a great deal of space to training and hyperparameter details, which risks obscuring the main contributions and detracting from readability.  
2. Key quantitative findings—particularly the ablation study results and the data in Figure 3—are largely confined to the appendix and receive minimal discussion in the main text. This placement makes it hard for readers to appreciate the full impact of stabilization methods.  
3. The discussion of broader limitations is somewhat narrow, focusing almost entirely on the interpretability of the discriminator. Other potential drawbacks (e.g., scalability to high‐dimensional PDEs or sensitivity to data noise) are not addressed.  
4. The stabilization strategies (instance noise scheduling and residual monitoring) are well known in the GAN literature. It remains unclear how much of the reported stability gains stem from these techniques versus other architectural or optimization choices.

Suggestions for Improvement  
- Reorganize the presentation by shifting detailed hyperparameter settings (currently in Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4) to the appendix. In parallel, bring the ablation study results (Section 5.2) and a concise summary of Figure 3 into the main text to highlight their importance.  
- Expand the discussion around Figure 3 in the results section. In particular, plot comparative simulation examples or error curves from classical PINNs and numerical solvers to contextualize the observed mean‐squared‐error improvements.  
- Broaden the limitations subsection to reflect potential challenges beyond discriminator interpretability, such as computational cost, extension to more complex PDE systems, or robustness to noisy boundary conditions.  
- Reframe the instance noise and residual early‐stopping mechanisms as “stable‐training strategies” rather than core algorithmic novelties. Provide insight into how these techniques interact with network depth, learning rates, or other design choices to give readers a clearer picture of their general applicability.

Minor Comments and Technical Corrections  
- Notation: The symbols G(x) and Ψₜ(x) appear to be used interchangeably in the loss definitions—please clarify.  
- Lines 179–181: The discriminator loss L_d and generator loss L_g are introduced without formal definition; adding explicit formulas would improve readability.  
- In Figure 3, the noise schedule σ = ReLU(L_g – L_d) is briefly mentioned but lacks a clear theoretical justification; consider adding a sentence to explain its rationale.

Decision: Accept (Rating: 7)  
Overall, this paper makes a substantial contribution by leveraging GANs to learn physics‐informed loss functions, demonstrating marked improvements over existing PINN and numerical approaches. Despite some organizational and framing issues around the stabilization techniques, the work is technically sound, experimentally thorough, and of high relevance to the scientific machine learning community. I therefore recommend acceptance.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper technically solid and well-structured, presenting a novel GAN-based approach to solve differential equations that outperforms classical PINNs and numerical solvers, but criticizes its readability, depth of discussion on certain results, and the novelty and framing of its stabilization techniques.
strengths: Well-written, well-structured, and easy to follow; clear description of methods and results
Ablation study and discussion of stabilization methods are interesting and relevant given GAN training challenges
Extensive experimental results convincingly demonstrate variability of classical PINNs and the advantage of using GANs over both PINNs and numerical solvers
weaknesses: Detailed training and hyperparameter sections distract from the main message and hinder readability
Key results (e.g., ablation study details and Figure 3) are relegated to the appendix and under‐described in the main text
Limited discussion of the method’s broader limitations beyond discriminator non-interpretability
Stabilization techniques (instance noise, residual monitoring) are not novel in the GAN literature and their effectiveness without other architectural choices is unclear
suggestions: Move detailed training/hyperparameter sections (3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4) to the appendix and bring the ablation study into the main paper; condense sections 3.3 and 4.1
Provide a more extensive discussion of Figure 3 and include comparative simulation examples from other methods to contextualize MSE improvements

main_points: GAN‐based loss learning replaces hand‐tuned PINN losses leading to improved empirical performance on ODEs and PDEs
GAN training is stabilized via instance noise and residual monitoring, addressing a known challenge
Classical PINNs with fixed L1, L2, and Huber losses show high variability and sometimes fail (e.g., modified Einstein equations)

Ablation study on stabilization techniques is described in Section 5.2 but mostly presented in the appendix
Figure 3 shows parallel runs with early termination via residual monitoring; linguistic/logical details on ReLU‐based noise scheduling (σ=ReLU(Lg−Ld)) are questioned
Notation issues: interchange of G(x) and Ψₜ(x), and undefined L_g and L_d in lines 179–181

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents DEQGAN, a novel unsupervised GAN-based framework for solving differential equations by learning the Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) loss function rather than relying on ground-truth solutions. Through adversarial training, the generator network produces candidate solutions while the discriminator learns to distinguish true residuals from generated ones. The authors integrate established GAN stabilization methods (e.g., instance noise, spectral normalization) to improve training robustness. Empirically, DEQGAN achieves several orders of magnitude improvement in solution accuracy compared to standard PINN loss functions such as L2, L1, and Huber, and the paper is complemented by extensive experimental details in a comprehensive appendix.

Strengths  
1. Novelty: Proposes an innovative unsupervised GAN approach to automatically learn a loss function tailored for differential-equation residuals, eliminating the need for ground-truth solution data.  
2. Empirical Gains: Demonstrates consistent, multiple-orders-of-magnitude improvements in solution accuracy across a variety of benchmark PDEs, outperforming classical PINN loss formulations.  
3. Robust Training: Adopts and carefully describes several GAN stabilization techniques in Section 3 (instance noise, spectral normalization, etc.), which appear to yield reliable convergence in practice.  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The manuscript is well written, and the appendix provides thorough details on network architectures, hyperparameters, and training procedures, facilitating reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Mode Collapse Concerns: The paper does not address the potential for GAN mode collapse or the generation of multiple or spurious solutions when learning the loss function in this setting. It remains unclear whether the adversarial framework can consistently avoid degenerate behavior.  
2. Lack of Theoretical Guarantees: There is no theoretical analysis or convergence proof regarding the correctness or stability of the learned loss function. As a result, one must rely solely on empirical evidence without understanding underlying guarantees.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Ablation Study on Stabilization Techniques: An ablation study quantifying the impact of each GAN “trick” (e.g., instance noise, spectral normalization) would help readers understand which components are most critical and how they interact.  
2. Mode Collapse Analysis: Including experiments or at least a discussion on mode collapse—such as evaluating solution diversity or tracking discriminator/generator loss dynamics—would strengthen confidence that DEQGAN consistently converges to valid physical solutions.

Technical Details  
- Generator and discriminator networks are trained adversarially to minimize the differential-equation residual through a learned loss function.  
- Section 3 outlines stabilization strategies: adding instance noise to inputs, applying spectral normalization to discriminator weights, and using gradient penalties.  
- Results compare DEQGAN against baseline PINN losses (L2, L1, Huber) on several PDE benchmarks, showing substantial accuracy gains.  
- No theoretical convergence proofs or formal analysis of potential failure modes (e.g., mode collapse) are provided.

Conclusion  
DEQGAN represents a significant technical contribution by introducing a data-free, adversarial approach to loss-function learning for physics-informed neural solvers. The empirical results are compelling, and the integration of stabilization methods is sensible and well documented. While further analysis on convergence and mode collapse would be valuable, these concerns do not diminish the paper’s overall impact and soundness. I therefore recommend acceptance.  

Rating: 7: Accept

reviews.summary: This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.
reviews.questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?
reviews.limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
reviews.rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.

Questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?

Limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.

Rating:
3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents DEQGAN, a physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework that learns its loss function via a generative adversarial network (GAN) rather than relying on fixed L1/L2 penalties. The authors demonstrate that DEQGAN outperforms standard PINNs on several benchmark PDEs, and they argue that a learned loss can adapt more flexibly to the underlying solution structure.

Strengths  
• Clarity and presentation: The manuscript is very well written, with a logical flow and clear explanations of the model architecture and training procedure.  
• Empirical gains: Across the tested PDEs, DEQGAN consistently yields lower residuals and higher solution accuracy compared to classical PINNs using L1 or L2 losses.  
• Innovative idea: Moving from predefined norms to a learned discrepancy measure is an interesting direction that could overcome some of the limitations of hand-tuned loss functions.

Weaknesses  
1. Incomplete positioning within related work  
   - Key adaptive-loss PINN methods such as McClenny & Braga-Neto’s SA-PINNs and Daw et al.’s PID-GAN are not discussed. As these papers also explore learned or adaptive discrepancy measures in the PINN context, omitting them weakens the novelty claim.  
2. Missing theoretical foundations  
   - The manuscript does not explain why standard L1/L2 losses fail in certain PDE learning tasks. A theoretical or empirical characterization of their shortcomings is needed to justify learning a more complex loss.  
   - There is no theoretical analysis of the proposed GAN-based loss. In particular, the classical GAN objective minimizes the Jensen–Shannon divergence under an optimal discriminator; in the deterministic PDE setting (where the data distribution is essentially a Dirac delta), this objective is ill-posed.  
   - When Gaussian instance noise is added to stabilize GAN training, minimizing the JS divergence with noise degenerates to an L2 criterion, calling into question what advantage the GAN formulation truly provides.  
3. Questionable applicability of GAN objectives  
   - GAN divergence measures assume overlap between discriminator and real data distributions. In a unique-solution PDE, the “real” neural output is a delta distribution, for which traditional GAN theory does not hold.  
4. Limited and potentially cherry-picked experiments  
   - Each PDE is tested with only a single artificial initial condition. Without multiple or more realistic conditions, it is difficult to assess robustness or generalization.  
   - The paper does not report comparisons on real-world or noisy PDE data, further limiting the scope.  
5. Training instability and reproducibility concerns  
   - The authors note that DEQGAN is unstable, requiring numerous independent runs and post-hoc filtering to select the “good” trials. This ad hoc procedure undermines reproducibility and practical applicability.  
   - There is no discussion of computational cost or time complexity relative to standard PINNs or to SA-PINNs/PID-GAN.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Related work and positioning  
   - Add a dedicated section discussing SA-PINNs (McClenny & Braga-Neto), PID-GAN (Daw et al.), and other adaptive-loss PINNs. Clearly delineate how DEQGAN differs.  
2. Theoretical justification  
   - Provide analysis or empirical evidence illustrating failure modes of L1/L2 losses in PINNs.  
   - Develop or cite theory explaining when and why a learned GAN loss can outperform simple norms, particularly addressing the unique-solution scenario and the role of instance noise.  
3. Clarify GAN objective choice  
   - Explain why minimizing a JS divergence via GAN training is preferable to direct L2 minimization, given that Gaussian noise often reduces GAN to L2.  
   - Discuss alternatives (e.g., Wasserstein GAN) if appropriate.  
4. Expand experimental validation  
   - Test DEQGAN on multiple and more varied initial/boundary conditions per PDE.  
   - Include experiments on real-world or noisy PDE data to demonstrate robustness.  
   - Compare wall-clock training time and iteration counts against baseline PINNs and adaptive-loss variants such as SA-PINNs and PID-GAN.  
5. Address stability and reproducibility  
   - Incorporate stabilization techniques (e.g., gradient penalties, learning-rate schedules) to reduce reliance on run filtering.  
   - Report variability metrics (e.g., mean and standard deviation over runs) rather than only best-case results.

Recommendation: Reject (Rating: 3)  
While DEQGAN is clearly presented and achieves notable empirical improvements over vanilla PINNs, the paper suffers from significant omissions in related work, lacks theoretical grounding for its core claim, misapplies GAN divergence objectives in a deterministic setting, and exhibits limited, unstable experimental evaluation. In its current form, these issues undermine both the scientific contribution and the reproducibility of the approach.

The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.

Weakness:

title: ISAAC Newton: Input-based Approximate Curvature for Newton's Method

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.
reviews.questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?
reviews.limitations: This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

Limitations:
This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper proposes a two-parameter Tikhonov‐regularized Gauss–Newton update within the K-FAC framework, introducing independent regularizers λ_g and λ_a. The authors show that by tuning these parameters one can smoothly interpolate between classical Gauss–Newton (small λ’s) and gradient descent (large λ’s), while guaranteeing a descent direction for all valid settings. Crucially, in the limit λ_g→∞ the update reduces to a highly efficient form ξ* that requires only layer‐input statistics and imposes negligible overhead. Empirical results on large neural architectures demonstrate that ξ* matches or outperforms strong baselines in training loss and test accuracy, while substantially cutting computation time. Overall, the paper is technically solid, well written, and offers both rigorous analysis and practical impact. I recommend acceptance.  

Strengths  
1. Novel and Practical Update Rule:  
   • The proposed ξ* update is both elegant and useful, yielding significantly lower computation time without compromising convergence or final performance.  
   • Validation on large‐scale networks confirms that ξ* scales well in practice.  

2. Rigorous Theoretical Treatment:  
   • The compact formulation in terms of λ_g and λ_a admits multiple interpretations, and the authors prove that every choice yields a descent direction.  
   • The derivation cleverly employs Kronecker‐factorized approximations and the Woodbury identity, culminating in clear guarantees on the update’s behavior.  

3. Clarity and Presentation:  
   • The manuscript is well organized and written in a clear, accessible style.  
   • The paper balances theoretical exposition with empirical evidence, making it easy to follow the motivation, derivation, and implementation details.  

Weakness  
– Limited Comparison to State‐of‐the‐Art  
  While the experiments convincingly show speedups over standard K-FAC and first‐order baselines, the paper does not benchmark against more recent optimizers (e.g., adaptive second‐order or quasi‐Newton schemes). This omission may slow adoption among practitioners.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Stronger Empirical Benchmarks  
   • Include comparisons to leading contemporary optimizers, such as Shampoo, L-BFGS variants, or recent adaptive second‐order methods, to demonstrate the competitiveness of ξ*.  

2. Typographical Clarification in Equation 6  
   • On line 70, the notation H appears to be misreferenced—please confirm whether this should denote the empirical GN matrix or another quantity.  

3. Intuitive Discussion of the Preconditioner M (Remark 2)  
   • While M is defined as M = (1/(b λ_g)) ĝ ĝᵀ, an intuitive explanation of its geometric effect on the update would aid readers.  
   • It would also be helpful to comment on whether this choice of M can reduce the number of iterations needed for convergence, beyond per‐iteration cost savings.  

Recommendation  
Accept (Rating: 7)  
This work delivers a technically sound, well‐motivated extension of K-FAC with strong theoretical guarantees and practical utility. With the suggested clarifications and additional benchmarks, it will serve as a valuable contribution to optimization for deep learning.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.

Questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

Limitations:
This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.
reviews.questions: See my comments above.
reviews.limitations: No
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.

Questions:
See my comments above.

No

reviews.text:
Summary  
This manuscript investigates a variant of the K-FAC optimizer in which the usual Tikhonov regularization terms on the Kronecker factors are decoupled, and one of them is driven to infinity. The authors show that, in this limit, the update reduces to vanilla SGD and provide a theoretical guarantee on the update direction. They also present empirical experiments on small-batch training to illustrate the interpolation between K-FAC and SGD induced by varying the regularization strengths. Overall, the paper is technically sound and clearly written, but its core contribution—namely, recovering SGD by an extreme choice of regularization—is of limited novelty, and the empirical study does not fully substantiate the authors’ claims.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Writing Quality  
   - The paper is very well written, with lucid exposition throughout.  
   - The preliminaries and algorithmic descriptions are detailed and easy to follow, making the method accessible.  

2. Theoretical Analysis  
   - Theorem 1 correctly characterizes the behavior of the update direction as the regularization parameter grows, and the derivation is rigorous.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Limited Novelty of the Main Contribution  
   - The central idea—that by making one Tikhonov regularizer arbitrarily large, K-FAC collapses to SGD—is a straightforward interpolation rather than a qualitatively new optimization scheme.  
   - Given the simplicity of this limit case, the theoretical guarantee on the update direction is unsurprising. To improve novelty, the authors might consider highlighting any unexpected consequences of this decoupling beyond the trivial interpolation.  

2. Insufficient Empirical Evaluation  
   - Missing Baselines: The experiments compare only K-FAC variants and vanilla SGD without momentum. Important optimizers such as Adam and SGD with momentum should be included to contextualize performance.  
   - Validation Curves: All reported figures focus solely on training loss. Presenting validation or test curves is crucial to support any claims about generalization.  
   - Hyperparameter Details: Key settings—especially weight decay for each optimizer—are not fully specified. A clear table of hyperparameters would improve reproducibility and credibility.  
   - Figure 1: The plotted curves showing loss versus the regularization strength simply mirror the expected interpolation between K-FAC and SGD. The authors could shorten or condense this figure and focus on more informative comparisons.  
   - Figure 2: Only training loss is shown, and there is no discussion of how hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates, weight decay) were tuned for each setting.  
   - Figure 6: The comparison uses pure SGD (no momentum), leading to unusually low CIFAR-10 accuracy. Since momentum is standard in CIFAR-10 training, the omission weakens the practical relevance of this result.  

3. Batch-Size Regime Justification  
   - The manuscript emphasizes performance in the small-batch regime but provides no experiments with larger batches. Without such comparisons, it is unclear why one would favor small-batch training over established large-batch methods. I recommend adding results for at least one large-batch setting to justify the focus.  

Recommendation (Rating: 5 – Borderline Accept)  
This work is technically solid and clearly articulated, and it offers a neat conceptual link between K-FAC and SGD via regularization control. However, its main algorithmic novelty is limited, and the empirical study requires significant expansion—particularly more relevant baselines, validation metrics, and full hyperparameter disclosure. Given the paper’s strengths in clarity and theoretical correctness, I lean toward acceptance, provided the authors address the evaluation concerns.

reviews.summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.
reviews.questions: What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review for “Two-Parameter Damping for Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature”  

Summary  
This paper proposes an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping used in K-FAC by introducing two independent regularization parameters—λ_g for the gradient factor and λ_a for the activity factor—and explores several limiting cases obtained by sending one of the parameters to zero or infinity. In particular, the “activity-only” variant (λ_g→∞) yields a simplified update (Equation 21) purported to reduce computational cost relative to the usual K-FAC update (Equation 13). The authors support their efficiency claims with an extensive empirical evaluation across multiple architectures and training settings. Some theoretical guarantees are provided, but these appear to follow directly from established properties of Tikhonov regularization and Kronecker-factored approximations.

Strengths  
1. Addresses an important and practical problem—how to make Gauss–Newton/Fisher-based optimizers more efficient in deep learning—building on a well-known baseline (K-FAC).  
2. The paper is clearly written for readers already familiar with Kronecker-factored approximations and Tikhonov damping; the notation around Equation 17 (the two-parameter update) is consistent and easy to follow.  
3. The experimental section is thorough: it evaluates runtime and optimization performance across several models and datasets, convincing the reader that the activity-only variant can indeed run faster in practice.

Weaknesses  
1. It is not clear what the paper’s main novelty is: is it the general two-parameter damping mechanism, or is it the special activity-only case? The distinction between these contributions should be sharper.  
2. While experiments demonstrate empirical speedups for the activity-only update, there is no theoretical runtime or complexity analysis comparing this method to standard K-FAC.  
3. Several of the theoretical results (e.g., basic spectral bounds and convergence implications of Tikhonov regularization) seem straightforward and offer limited new insight into the behavior of the modified update.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clearly state early on whether the primary contribution is (a) the general two-parameter damping framework (λ_g and λ_a), or (b) the discovery and analysis of the activity-only special case. If both are important, motivate their distinct roles more explicitly.  
2. Add a theoretical complexity analysis (e.g., flops or memory cost) contrasting the activity-only update (Equation 21) with the standard K-FAC approximation (Equation 13). This would strengthen the claims about reduced computational overhead.  
3. Provide more intuition for why Equation 21 is expected to approximate the full two-parameter update well. In particular, explain the circumstances under which dropping the gradient factor (λ_g→∞) remains a good surrogate for the original Kronecker-factored curvature correction.

Overall Assessment and Recommendation  
This work tackles a relevant challenge in scalable second-order optimization and pairs a neat generalization of K-FAC’s damping with solid empirical results. However, the paper would benefit from a clearer statement of its core contribution and a theoretical complexity comparison to back up the runtime claims. On balance, the technical strengths and thorough experiments outweigh these concerns. I recommend a borderline accept (Rating: 5), as the paper is technically sound and of interest to the community, provided the authors clarify and tighten the theoretical presentation.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4].

Summary:
Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

[1] Gupta,V.,Koren,T., Singer,Y.:, Shampoo: Preconditioned stochastic tensor optimization. In: International Conference on Machine Learning.

[2] Goldfarb, D., Ren, Y., Bahamou, A.:, Practical quasi-Newton methods for training deep neural networks. In:Advances inNeural Information Processing Systems.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.

reviews.text:
Summary  
The manuscript proposes ISAAC, a novel variant of the Kronecker-factored Gauss–Newton (GGN) approximation that introduces two independent regularization parameters. By varying these parameters, ISAAC interpolates between classical K-FAC (when both regularizers vanish) and vanilla gradient descent (when they become large), while also uncovering a new, computationally cheap update direction. Crucially, the efficient implementation requires only the layer input covariance—avoiding the costly computation of the curvature term ¯g—and needs to invert just a single matrix. The authors provide theoretical analysis of the spectral properties of the method and prove that the update direction aligns with that of the full GGN under mild positive semi-definiteness assumptions.

Strengths  
1. The introduction of two disentangled regularization parameters in the Kronecker-factored GGN yields a flexible family of optimizers, unifying K-FAC, gradient descent, and intermediate schemes.  
2. The efficient variant of ISAAC relies solely on the input covariance factor, eliminating the need for an extra back-propagation to compute ¯g and thus lowering computational cost compared to standard K-FAC.  
3. Theoretical analysis carefully characterizes the spectrum of the approximate GGN and establishes that the ISAAC update direction matches that of the full GGN under regularization.  
4. The work clearly demonstrates that, in appropriate limits, ISAAC recovers well-known methods, bolstering our understanding of the relationship between first- and second-order techniques.

Weaknesses  
1. Readability and presentation suffer from several formatting and notation issues: missing brackets in equations (16) and (17), omitted division by batch size in Eq. (19), and non-standard reference grouping (e.g., “[14,15]”).  
2. Several recent and relevant second-order optimizers—such as Shampoo, KBFGS, Natural Gradient Plus (NG+), and SENG—are neither cited nor discussed, leaving the related-work section incomplete.  
3. Some of the stated theoretical results (e.g., Theorem 2 on direction correctness) follow immediately from the positive semi-definiteness of the regularized factors and lack deeper insight or a full convergence proof. The absence of a formal algorithm box and convergence guarantees further limits the theoretical contribution.  
4. Empirical evaluation is restricted to small-scale settings; large-scale benchmarks (e.g., ResNet-50 on ImageNet-1k) and comparisons with modern second-order optimizers are missing, making it difficult to assess practical impact.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Polish the manuscript for clarity: fix equation formatting (brackets, division by batch size), standardize reference citations, and ensure notational consistency.  
• Enrich the related-work discussion by citing and contrasting ISAAC with Shampoo, KBFGS, NG+, and SENG, and clarify how ISAAC differs in computational complexity and convergence behavior.  
• Provide a formal algorithmic description of ISAAC (in pseudocode or algorithm box) and, if possible, outline convergence guarantees or conditions under which such guarantees hold.  
• Extend the experimental section with large-scale benchmarks (for example, training ResNet-50 on ImageNet-1k) and include comparisons against leading second-order methods to demonstrate empirical advantages.  
• Discuss the relationship between the GGN approximation used here and the Fisher information matrix, situating ISAAC within the broader context of natural gradient methods.  
• Clarify whether the same or different mini-batches can be used for estimating the GGN factors and for computing the gradient, and analyze the theoretical impact of using distinct batches.  
• Consider broadening the theoretical analysis to other architectures, such as convolutional layers, to enhance the general applicability of the method.

Technical Comments  
– Eq. (19) should include the division by batch size b in the gradient term gᵀa/b.  
– Theorem 2’s proof is immediate once one notes that (λI + factor)⁻¹ is PSD; consider providing deeper insights or alternative justifications.  
– The empirical claim that aaᵀ dominates ¯gᵀ¯g in the Kronecker approximation is stated without theoretical support; a brief discussion or reference would strengthen this point.

Recommendation  
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the proposed ISAAC method a novel and efficient Kronecker-factored Gauss-Newton variant that uses only layer inputs and two independent regularization parameters, recovering K-FAC or gradient descent in the limits, but notes the presentation needs polishing, missing related work, and more extensive evaluation.
strengths: Introduces two independent regularization parameters in the Kronecker-factored GGN approximation.
Method recovers classical K-FAC when parameters vanish and gradient descent when they grow.
Computationally more efficient than K-FAC by inverting only one matrix and avoiding extra backpropagation for computing ¯g.
Provides theoretical analysis showing the spectrum of methods and direction correctness.
weaknesses: Manuscript readability needs improvement; the paper should be polished.
Lacks citation of several relevant second-order methods (Shampoo, KBFGS, NG+, SENG).
Some theoretical results are straightforward or trivial and lack a formal algorithmic presentation and convergence proof.
Evaluation is limited; no large-scale experiments (e.g., ResNet50 on ImageNet-1k) or comparisons to other modern second-order optimizers.
suggestions: Improve paper clarity and polish the writing for easier reading.
Add citations and discussion of related second-order methods such as Shampoo, KBFGS, NG+, and SENG.
Include a formal algorithm box and provide convergence guarantees for the proposed method.
Augment experiments with large-scale benchmarks (e.g., ResNet50 on ImageNet-1k) and compare against other second-order optimizers.
Discuss the relation between the GGN matrix and the Fisher information matrix in the context of existing literature.
Fix formatting and notation issues: bracket placement in equations (16) and (17), reference grouping (e.g., [14,15]), and include the missing division by batch size in Eq. (19).
Clarify whether different batches can be used for GGN approximation and gradient computation and the impact on theoretical properties.
Consider extending theoretical analysis to convolutional neural networks.
main_points: Disentangling the two regularization parameters in the Kronecker-factored GGN yields a family of update methods including K-FAC and gradient descent.
The efficient variant uses only the input covariance factor, eliminating the need to compute the costly curvature term ¯g.
By varying regularization strength, one recovers classical methods and discovers a new inexpensive update direction.
Theorem 2 (direction correctness) is trivial under the positive semi-definiteness of the regularized factors.
The work currently focuses on fully-connected layers; extension to other architectures is not addressed.
Important recent works in second-order optimization are not discussed.
technical_details: Equation (19) omits the division by batch size b in the gradient term gᵀa/b.
The derivation assumes using the same batch for GGN approximation and gradient; implications of using different batches are not explored.
Theorem 2 relies on the PSD property of (λI + factor) inverses, making the ascent-direction result immediate.
Formatting issues: missing brackets around η and the gradient in Eqs. (16) and (17); combined reference formatting at line 60.
Empirical claim that aaᵀ dominates ¯gᵀ¯g in the Kronecker approximation is noted but not theoretically justified.

title: LeRaC: Learning Rate Curriculum

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).
reviews.questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.
reviews.limitations: This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
