reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes a per-layer learning rate schedule intended to improve generalization in deep networks. The authors frame their approach as a form of curriculum learning: they gradually “warm up” lower layers by assigning them smaller initial rates and then increasing these rates exponentially (Eq. 8) over k iterations. Empirically, the method is simple to implement, yields clear gains in validation performance across several benchmarks, and outperforms a fixed, global learning rate schedule in terms of final generalization.

Strengths  
• Clarity and readability: The method is explained in straightforward terms, with pseudo-code and a clear description of the exponential scheduler.  
• Simplicity: Implementation requires only per-layer scaling of an existing optimizer, making it easy for practitioners to adopt.  
• Empirical generalization gains: The paper presents consistent improvements in held-out accuracy and validation loss versus a baseline global-rate schedule.

Weaknesses  
1. Framing as curriculum learning.  
   The approach appears more akin to an optimization technique—specifically, a per-layer learning rate routine—than a curriculum on the data or tasks. This framing may obscure connections to the rich optimization literature.  
2. Missing comparisons to adaptive optimizers.  
   Standard adaptive methods (e.g., Adam, Adadelta [Zeiler 2012], SM3) inherently produce layer-dependent effective rates. The paper does not compare against these, nor does it report their per-layer rates for a fair side-by-side.  
3. Lack of training-loss curves.  
   Only validation loss and error over time are shown. Without training-loss curves, it is difficult to disentangle whether generalization gains come from faster convergence or from a genuine curriculum effect.  
4. Unjustified scheduler choice and hyperparameters.  
   The exponential form of Eq. 8 (base c=10) and initial rates νₙ⁽⁰⁾ down to five or six orders of magnitude are asserted without empirical comparison to simpler schedules (e.g., linear warm-up) or discussion of how c, νₙ⁽⁰⁾, and k were tuned.  
5. Early stopping and total steps underspecified.  
   It is unclear how the authors determined the total number of training iterations, the warm-up length k, and the learning-rate restarts or plateaus for early stopping. These choices can significantly influence results.  
6. Vague noise-reduction argument.  
   The claim that untrained layers introduce “noise” and that the schedule mitigates this effect is introduced without measurement or visualization. There is no quantitative evidence for noise propagation from random initialization (cited via [21]).

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Expand related work.  
   Include discussion of per-layer adaptation in optimizers such as Adadelta (Zeiler 2012), Adam, Adafactor, AdamW, and SM3 (NeurIPS ’19). Outline how effective average learning rates per layer are defined in that literature and relate these to your schedule.  
2. Compare to adaptive optimizers.  
   Run experiments with Adam, Adadelta, and SM3, reporting both their global performance and the effective per-layer rates over time. This will show whether the proposed exponential schedule offers benefits beyond existing adaptive strategies.  
3. Report training-loss curves.  
   Plot training and validation loss (and accuracy) against wall-time or iterations for both your method and baselines. This will clarify if the validation improvements stem from faster optimization or true generalization effects.  
4. Justify or explore scheduler variants.  
   Provide an ablation comparing the exponential warm-up to other schedules (e.g., linear, polynomial). Discuss how the base c=10, initial νₙ⁽⁰⁾ values, and warm-up length k were chosen (grid search, heuristics, etc.).  
5. Detail early stopping criteria.  
   Describe the protocol for selecting total training steps and how learning-rate plateaus or restarts trigger early stopping. If possible, evaluate sensitivity to these choices.  
6. Quantify noise reduction.  
   Propose a metric or visualization (e.g., norm of gradient noise per layer, variance of activations) to substantiate the claim that untrained layers inject harmful variance and that your schedule alleviates this issue.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work presents a technically sound, easy-to-implement per-layer learning rate schedule with promising empirical improvements. However, the manuscript would benefit from deeper engagement with existing optimization literature, clearer experimental validation (including adaptive baselines and training-loss curves), and justification for key design choices. With these revisions, the paper could make a stronger case for the novelty and broader applicability of the proposed scheduler.  

Rating: 6: Weak Accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).

Questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.

Limitations:
This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes setting different learning rates for each layer of a deep neural network such that the first layers (closer to the input) have a much higher learning rate than the last layers. Learning rates are all gradually increased until they reach the same value $\eta_0$ (the optimal learning rate of no-curriculum models).
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- Simple, novel, and effective method. 
- Good review of related work and reasoning for using model-level curriculum learning.
- Consistent performance improvements across eight audio, image, and text datasets and seven convolutional, recurrent, and transformer architectures. 


Weaknesses:
- Insufficient analysis on why the method works. 
- The motivation for setting different learning rates per layer is not sound. The authors state that random initialization of parameters causes the propagation of noise during the forward pass, and that assigning higher learning rate to first layers and lower learning rates to last layers prevents this propagation of noise. But there is no proof for this argument.
reviews.questions:
- What is the method for optimizing $\eta_j$, j = {1 ... n}?
- How sensitive is the model to different settings of $\eta_j$?
- How do the training dynamics compare to standard training where all layers are trained at the same rate? Would the earlier layers generate qualitatively different features maps than under-trained later layers?
reviews.limitations:
- The interaction of the model with adaptive optimizers that scale the learning rate of each parameter is not well justified. 
- Learning rate schedulers may disturb the proposed model. If the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer $\eta_n$ is sufficiently increased, the last layer may never be trained enough, training may take much longer or may become unstable.

Summary:
This paper proposes setting different learning rates for each layer of a deep neural network such that the first layers (closer to the input) have a much higher learning rate than the last layers. Learning rates are all gradually increased until they reach the same value $\eta_0$ (the optimal learning rate of no-curriculum models).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- Simple, novel, and effective method. 
- Good review of related work and reasoning for using model-level curriculum learning.
- Consistent performance improvements across eight audio, image, and text datasets and seven convolutional, recurrent, and transformer architectures. 


Weaknesses:
- Insufficient analysis on why the method works. 
- The motivation for setting different learning rates per layer is not sound. The authors state that random initialization of parameters causes the propagation of noise during the forward pass, and that assigning higher learning rate to first layers and lower learning rates to last layers prevents this propagation of noise. But there is no proof for this argument.

Questions:
- What is the method for optimizing $\eta_j$, j = {1 ... n}?
- How sensitive is the model to different settings of $\eta_j$?
- How do the training dynamics compare to standard training where all layers are trained at the same rate? Would the earlier layers generate qualitatively different features maps than under-trained later layers?

Limitations:
- The interaction of the model with adaptive optimizers that scale the learning rate of each parameter is not well justified. 
- Learning rate schedulers may disturb the proposed model. If the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer $\eta_n$ is sufficiently increased, the last layer may never be trained enough, training may take much longer or may become unstable.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “Model-Level Curriculum Learning via Layer-Wise Learning-Rate Scheduling”

Summary  
This paper proposes a simple yet effective model-level curriculum learning strategy by assigning different initial learning rates to each layer in a network—higher rates in early layers and lower rates in later layers—that all gradually converge to a common base rate η₀. The method is domain-agnostic and demonstrates consistent accuracy gains across eight audio, image, and text benchmarks and seven distinct architectures. However, the core motivation—namely, that layer-wise rate scheduling mitigates noise propagation from random initialization—lacks theoretical justification or detailed empirical analysis. Overall, the paper is technically sound and offers a novel contribution, meriting a Weak Accept.

Strengths  
1. Simplicity and Novelty: The proposed curriculum is straightforward to implement, requires no architectural changes, and introduces a fresh perspective on layer-wise learning dynamics.  
2. Broad Empirical Validation: Experiments on eight datasets spanning audio, vision, and NLP tasks, as well as evaluations on seven network backbones, consistently show performance improvements over uniform learning-rate baselines.  
3. Comprehensive Related Work: The manuscript surveys prior curriculum learning approaches and clearly positions the proposed model-level curriculum within existing literature, providing a strong rationale for its design.

Weaknesses  
1. Lack of Theoretical/Empirical Analysis: The central claim that layer-wise scheduling reduces “noise propagation” from random initialization is asserted but not rigorously analyzed or demonstrated.  
2. Hyperparameter Optimization Details Omitted: The process by which the per-layer initial learning rates η_j (for j = 1…n) are chosen or tuned is not described, leaving questions about reproducibility.  
3. Sensitivity to η_j Settings: There is no study of how sensitive the method’s gains are to different layer-wise rate configurations or how robust the approach is across hyperparameter choices.  
4. Interaction with Adaptive Optimizers: Modern optimizers (e.g., Adam, RMSProp) internally scale per-parameter learning rates. It is unclear whether layer-wise scheduling complements or conflicts with such adaptive schemes.  
5. Learning-Rate Scheduler Compatibility: Standard decay schedules may reduce the base rate η₀ before later layers have fully “caught up,” potentially undermining the intended curriculum; this interaction is not discussed or evaluated.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical or Empirical Support for Noise Argument: Include either a formal analysis or controlled experiments (e.g., measuring gradient variance or signal-to-noise ratio across layers) to substantiate the noise-propagation motivation.  
2. Clarify η_j Optimization: Detail the search or optimization procedure used to select initial layer-wise rates, including ranges, validation protocol, and any automated tuning methods.  
3. Sensitivity Analysis: Report experiments that vary the η_j schedule (e.g., different slopes, non-linear interpolations to η₀) to demonstrate robustness or identify optimal configurations.  
4. Training Dynamics Comparison: Provide side-by-side analyses of training trajectories and feature representations (e.g., layer activations or learned filters) under uniform versus curriculum scheduling to illustrate the qualitative impact of the method.  
5. Adaptive Optimizer Compatibility: Evaluate the approach with at least one adaptive optimizer and discuss how its internal rate scaling interacts with the proposed curriculum, possibly proposing adjustments if conflicts arise.  
6. Scheduler Configuration Guidance: Offer recommendations on how to set learning-rate decay milestones or warm-up periods to ensure that later layers receive sufficient training at higher relative rates before convergence.

Recommendation  
Weak Accept (Rating: 6). The paper introduces a novel, easy-to-apply technique with consistently positive empirical results across multiple domains. While the lack of in-depth analysis of the core motivation and certain methodological details prevents a stronger endorsement, the contribution is substantial and likely to inspire further investigation in curriculum learning at the model level.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes setting different learning rates for each layer of a deep neural network such that the first layers (closer to the input) have a much higher learning rate than the last layers. Learning rates are all gradually increased until they reach the same value $\eta_0$ (the optimal learning rate of no-curriculum models).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- Simple, novel, and effective method. 
- Good review of related work and reasoning for using model-level curriculum learning.
- Consistent performance improvements across eight audio, image, and text datasets and seven convolutional, recurrent, and transformer architectures. 


Weaknesses:
- Insufficient analysis on why the method works. 
- The motivation for setting different learning rates per layer is not sound. The authors state that random initialization of parameters causes the propagation of noise during the forward pass, and that assigning higher learning rate to first layers and lower learning rates to last layers prevents this propagation of noise. But there is no proof for this argument.

Questions:
- What is the method for optimizing $\eta_j$, j = {1 ... n}?
- How sensitive is the model to different settings of $\eta_j$?
- How do the training dynamics compare to standard training where all layers are trained at the same rate? Would the earlier layers generate qualitatively different features maps than under-trained later layers?

Limitations:
- The interaction of the model with adaptive optimizers that scale the learning rate of each parameter is not well justified. 
- Learning rate schedulers may disturb the proposed model. If the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer $\eta_n$ is sufficiently increased, the last layer may never be trained enough, training may take much longer or may become unstable.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015
reviews.questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.
reviews.limitations: The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015

Questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.

Limitations:
The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces a novel layer-wise learning rate curriculum in which each layer’s learning rate is initialized differently—shallow layers receive higher rates—and then all rates are exponentially increased until they equalize over a fixed schedule. The authors empirically validate this strategy across multiple domains (vision, NLP) and architectures, showing consistent improvements over standard uniform-rate baselines. The writing is clear and the experiments are thorough. However, the manuscript leaves several conceptual and methodological questions unanswered: the connection to curriculum learning is underdeveloped, existing adaptive and warm-up learning rate methods are not sufficiently discussed, there is no theoretical or intuitive rationale for the schedule, and the additional hyperparameters raise concerns about sensitivity and reproducibility.  

Strengths  
• Novelty: Proposing a layer-wise curriculum for learning rates is an interesting and original idea that departs from uniform or globally adaptive schemes.  
• Empirical validation: The experimental suite is extensive, covering different tasks and network families, which convincingly demonstrates the generality of the approach.  
• Clarity: The paper is well written, logically organized, and easy to follow.  

Weaknesses  
• Curriculum Learning Link: The claim that this is a curriculum-learning method remains vague—there is little discussion of how “easy-to-hard” principles apply at the layer level or how this strategy parallels data/model-level curricula.  
• Incomplete Related Work: Key prior work on layer-specific or adaptive learning rates (e.g., Singh et al. 2015; Ginsburg et al. 2018) and popular warm-up schedules (e.g., Gotmare et al. 2018; You et al. 2017) are omitted, making it hard to position the contribution.  
• Lack of Theoretical/Intuitive Rationale: No justification is provided for why exponentially increasing per-layer rates until they match is effective, beyond empirical observation.  
• Additional Hyperparameters: The method introduces new settings (initial per-layer rates η⁽⁰⁾_j, schedule length k) without guidance on selection or analysis of sensitivity, raising reproducibility concerns.  
• Conflict with Fine-tuning Practice: The proposed scheme (higher initial rates on shallow layers) appears at odds with conventional wisdom in transfer learning, where deeper layers often use higher rates; this tension is not addressed.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify the Curriculum Connection: Explicitly discuss how layer-wise rate progression embodies “learning from easy to hard,” and compare against both data-based and model-based curriculum frameworks.  
2. Expand Related Work: Incorporate and contrast your approach with existing layer- or module-specific adaptive LR methods (Singh et al. 2015; Ginsburg et al. 2018) and warm-up heuristics (Gotmare et al. 2018; You et al. 2017), emphasizing the novelty of your schedule.  
3. Provide Rationale or Theory: Offer an intuitive argument or, ideally, a theoretical analysis for why ramping layer rates until they equalize yields better optimization dynamics.  
4. Analyze Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Present experiments or guidelines on choosing η⁽⁰⁾_j and k, showing how performance varies under different settings.  
5. Visualize the Schedule: Include a plot of μ(j) (the per-layer rate) over training iterations to make Eq. (8) more transparent to readers.  
6. Address Fine-tuning Contradiction: Discuss why your findings diverge from standard practices in transfer learning, and whether your curriculum can be reconciled with layer-freezing or differential-rate strategies.  

Minor Technical Comments  
– In Section 3.2, clarify the units and typical magnitudes of η⁽⁰⁾_j and k.  
– State whether you use the same schedule across all tasks or tune it per dataset.  
– Provide pseudocode for the scheduler to aid reproducibility.  

Decision: 5 – Borderline Accept  
The proposed layer-wise learning rate curriculum is a technically solid and empirically well-supported contribution. Its novelty and broad applicability are clear strengths. However, the paper would benefit greatly from a deeper conceptual grounding, richer engagement with related work, and practical guidance on hyperparameter tuning. I recommend acceptance on the condition that the authors substantially address the points above to strengthen both the theoretical and methodological presentation.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015

Questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.

Limitations:
The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer appreciates the novel layer-wise learning rate curriculum and its broad empirical validation, but finds its connection to curriculum learning unclear, notes missing theoretical justification, and highlights gaps in related work and methodological clarity.
strengths: The layer-wise learning rate strategy is an important and novel idea.
Empirical experiments are comprehensive and demonstrate generality across domains and architectures.
The paper is well written and easy to read.
weaknesses: The link between the proposed method and curriculum learning is vague and not well motivated.
Related work discussion is insufficient—missing comparisons to existing layer-wise or adaptive learning rate methods and to warm-up strategies.
Lacks theoretical analysis or intuitive explanation for why increasing learning rates until they equalize is reasonable.
The mechanism is not intuitive and relies on more hyperparameters than conventional training methods, raising concerns about sensitivity and justification.
suggestions: Clarify how this strategy fits within the curriculum learning paradigm and compare more directly to data- and model-level curriculum methods.

Expand related work to include and contrast with layer-specific adaptive learning rate approaches (e.g., Singh et al. 2015; Ginsburg et al. 2018) and warm-up techniques (e.g., Gotmare et al. 2018; You et al. 2017).
Provide theoretical justification or intuitive explanation for the chosen learning rate schedule and its benefits.
Discuss hyperparameter selection and sensitivity—how to choose initial layer rates and schedule length.
Include a plot of the layer-wise learning rate schedule (μ(j) over iterations) to make Eq. (8) more transparent.
Explain the apparent contradiction between the proposed high-rate-for-shallow-layers scheme and common fine-tuning practice where deep layers often use higher rates.
main_points: The proposed method's relation to classical curriculum learning (learning from easy to hard) is not clearly established.
Important prior work on layer-wise/adaptive learning rates and warm-up heuristics is omitted, making the contribution boundary unclear.
There is no theoretical or intuitive explanation for why the learning rates should ramp until equal across layers.
The design introduces additional hyperparameters whose tuning and sensitivity are not addressed.
The strategy seems at odds with typical fine-tuning approaches for pre-trained models.
technical_details: Hyperparameter choices: initial per-layer learning rates (η^(0)_j) and the schedule length k are not guided or experimentally analyzed for sensitivity.
Eq. (8) defines an exponential increase in per-layer learning rates; a plot of μ(j) over iterations would aid understanding.
The reviewer questions how to set and tune the layer-wise initial rates and schedule hyperparameters in practice.
For pre-trained models, the usual practice is low rates for early layers and higher rates for later layers—this conflict needs technical clarification.
The mechanism adds more hyperparameters compared to conventional regimes, raising concerns about reproducibility and robustness.

reviews.summary: The manuscript proposes a model-level curriculum learning framework that uses variable learning rates across a DNN to jump-start the learning process. The core idea revolves around compensating for the difference in learning across the depth of a DNN by using larger learning rates closer to the input and gradually decreasing their value towards the output layers. As intended, this curriculum is data-free and removes the need to assess the difficulty of samples, similar to how data-level curriculum operates, and instead can be applied across a range of architectures and tasks. The proposed idea improves on the performance of standard training regimes across vision, language and audio tasks, using residual, recurrent and transformer-based architectures.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The writing offers a clear and understandable representation of the curriculum learning approach.  More importantly, the categorization of various levels of curriculum learning provides important context to the ideas being discussed.
- The depth of experimental evaluation, stretching across datasets, tasks and architectures is commendable.

Weaknesses
- Data augmentation has proven valuable to the training process, be it commonly used approaches like random cropping/flipping or more intricate mixed approaches. They offer substantial improvement in performance and as well as alternative properties of a DNN. The proposed method is not evaluated alongside any common preprocessing techniques (vision datasets), thus offering a gap in the exploration of the behavior of the proposed algorithm alongside them.
- The set of results posted across various tables in the manuscript is commendable. However, in certain cases the the average performance and standard deviation across multiple methods are extremely close. While this does not take away from the final conclusions, the use of significance tests/metrics could help further distinguish improvements.
- The descriptions of the preprocessing and training regimes offer extremely important context to the results presented throughout the paper.
However, an exploration of the two key parameters that make up the proposed method, various update setting for the learning rate and the choice of iteration/epoch at which to converge to the standard learning rate would offer more insight into the proposed method.
reviews.questions:
- Specific to the vision datasets and results, could the authors provide insights into (a) how the use of common preprocessing techniques affects the behavior of the proposed approach, and (b) the balance in peak performance achievable in standard training vs. the proposed method when preprocessing is included. While preprocessing techniques add some element of stochasticity, it is important to understand an algorithm in that context.
- The addition of significance tests/metrics would be beneficial to further emphasize the benefits of the proposed algorithm as well as other areas for potential improvement. Could the authors reassess their results using significance metrics?
- Moving a small portion of the experimental setup to the appendices/supplementary material and including a study of how k (iterations/epochs), from small to absurdly large, affects the improvement in performance offered by the proposed algorithm would add another dimension to the evaluations presented in this work. The results themselves should be a by-product of the suite of experiments already executed so hopefully there is minimal overhead.
- Given the variable levels of improvement, even within the scope of the same task, could the authors discuss plausible schemes that take into account other factors (like data, gradient flow, etc.) that could provide more consistent gains?
- At a more abstract level could the authors address the potential negative implications/limitations of their approach.
reviews.limitations:
While there aren't glaring limitations or issues with the proposed method, the authors can consider potential negative effects in alternative properties of the DNN, as a by-product.
Since there isn't an evaluation of the calibration quality or adversarial robustness of the final solutions, these could be potential issues or limitations to the proposed algorithm. 
Keeping robust/adversarial training or alternative objective functions to classification in mind could lead to unexpected outcomes.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The manuscript proposes a model-level curriculum learning framework that uses variable learning rates across a DNN to jump-start the learning process. The core idea revolves around compensating for the difference in learning across the depth of a DNN by using larger learning rates closer to the input and gradually decreasing their value towards the output layers. As intended, this curriculum is data-free and removes the need to assess the difficulty of samples, similar to how data-level curriculum operates, and instead can be applied across a range of architectures and tasks. The proposed idea improves on the performance of standard training regimes across vision, language and audio tasks, using residual, recurrent and transformer-based architectures.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The writing offers a clear and understandable representation of the curriculum learning approach.  More importantly, the categorization of various levels of curriculum learning provides important context to the ideas being discussed.
- The depth of experimental evaluation, stretching across datasets, tasks and architectures is commendable.

Weaknesses
- Data augmentation has proven valuable to the training process, be it commonly used approaches like random cropping/flipping or more intricate mixed approaches. They offer substantial improvement in performance and as well as alternative properties of a DNN. The proposed method is not evaluated alongside any common preprocessing techniques (vision datasets), thus offering a gap in the exploration of the behavior of the proposed algorithm alongside them.
- The set of results posted across various tables in the manuscript is commendable. However, in certain cases the the average performance and standard deviation across multiple methods are extremely close. While this does not take away from the final conclusions, the use of significance tests/metrics could help further distinguish improvements.
- The descriptions of the preprocessing and training regimes offer extremely important context to the results presented throughout the paper.
However, an exploration of the two key parameters that make up the proposed method, various update setting for the learning rate and the choice of iteration/epoch at which to converge to the standard learning rate would offer more insight into the proposed method.

Questions:
- Specific to the vision datasets and results, could the authors provide insights into (a) how the use of common preprocessing techniques affects the behavior of the proposed approach, and (b) the balance in peak performance achievable in standard training vs. the proposed method when preprocessing is included. While preprocessing techniques add some element of stochasticity, it is important to understand an algorithm in that context.
- The addition of significance tests/metrics would be beneficial to further emphasize the benefits of the proposed algorithm as well as other areas for potential improvement. Could the authors reassess their results using significance metrics?
- Moving a small portion of the experimental setup to the appendices/supplementary material and including a study of how k (iterations/epochs), from small to absurdly large, affects the improvement in performance offered by the proposed algorithm would add another dimension to the evaluations presented in this work. The results themselves should be a by-product of the suite of experiments already executed so hopefully there is minimal overhead.
- Given the variable levels of improvement, even within the scope of the same task, could the authors discuss plausible schemes that take into account other factors (like data, gradient flow, etc.) that could provide more consistent gains?
- At a more abstract level could the authors address the potential negative implications/limitations of their approach.

Limitations:
While there aren't glaring limitations or issues with the proposed method, the authors can consider potential negative effects in alternative properties of the DNN, as a by-product.
Since there isn't an evaluation of the calibration quality or adversarial robustness of the final solutions, these could be potential issues or limitations to the proposed algorithm. 
Keeping robust/adversarial training or alternative objective functions to classification in mind could lead to unexpected outcomes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces LeRaC, a model-level curriculum learning framework that dynamically adjusts the learning rate to guide training progression. The method is clearly described and placed within the broader taxonomy of curriculum approaches. Empirical results on vision, language, and audio benchmarks, using diverse architectures (residual, recurrent, transformer), demonstrate consistent, albeit modest, performance gains over strong baselines.

Strengths  
• Clarity of presentation: The authors provide a well-structured description of LeRaC and clearly situate it relative to existing curriculum learning methods.  
• Broad evaluation: Experiments span multiple domains (computer vision, NLP, audio) and architectures, illustrating the generality of the approach. The gains are reported across standard datasets and model types, lending credibility to the claimed improvements.

Weaknesses  
• Interaction with data augmentation: All vision experiments rely on basic preprocessing, but leave out commonly used augmentations (e.g., random cropping, horizontal flipping, mixup). This raises questions about how LeRaC behaves in realistic training pipelines.  
• Statistical significance: Reported accuracy gains are often comparable in magnitude to the standard deviations. Without formal significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals), it is difficult to judge whether these differences are reliable.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity: Two critical LeRaC settings—the learning-rate update schedule (linear vs. exponential) and the number of warm-up iterations/epochs (k)—are introduced but neither ablated nor justified.  
• Discussion of limitations: The paper does not consider potential adverse effects of the curriculum, such as its impact on model calibration, adversarial robustness, or applicability to non-classification objectives.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Augmentation studies: Incorporate LeRaC into standard vision augmentation pipelines (random crops, flips, mixup) to assess whether its benefits persist under typical training regimes.  
2. Statistical analysis: Report statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests) or confidence intervals alongside mean accuracy and standard deviation to distinguish meaningful gains from noise.  
3. Hyperparameter ablation: Include a sensitivity analysis—perhaps in an appendix—varying the learning-rate schedule (linear vs. exponential) and the convergence iteration k to justify the chosen settings.  
4. Supplementary material: Move detailed descriptions of training protocols and preprocessing steps to the supplementary section to free space for additional ablations and analysis.  
5. Adaptive schemes: Discuss or prototype more data-driven approaches (e.g., gradient-based criteria or sample difficulty measures) that could potentially yield more consistent improvements across tasks.  
6. Limitations and reliability: Add a discussion of possible negative consequences, including effects on calibration quality and adversarial robustness, and outline directions for evaluating these aspects in future work.

Conclusion  
LeRaC is a technically sound and broadly applicable curriculum learning framework, and the paper offers a clear exposition and extensive empirical evaluation. However, key questions remain about its interaction with standard data augmentations, the statistical robustness of its gains, hyperparameter choices, and potential limitations. These gaps prevent full confidence in the generality and reliability of the approach. On balance, the paper merits acceptance, provided the authors address the above concerns in a revision. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: Borderline accept.

title: The Slingshot Mechanism: An Empirical Study of Adaptive Optimizers and the \emph{Grokking Phenomenon}

reviews.summary: The authors describe a "slingshot" effect present in the use of Adam (and cousins). This purportedly cyclic effect can be observed when the norm of the final layer sharply increases, often accompanied by an increase in train loss. The authors offer the slingshot effect as a possible explanation for the ability of Adam to induce generalization even without explicit regularization.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This is overall a nicely executed empirical paper that reveals a moderately interesting phenomenon. A cyclic regime of generalization would be quite remarkable and this paper offers preliminary evidence that supports the authors' hypothesis. It is also refreshing that the authors did not attempt to handwave nonsense poorly-motivated math into the paper, which is unfortunately a severe problem even in 2022.

My main concerns are the following:

- Significance: Given that this effect explains generalization in unregularized optimization, the overall impact to practical deep learning is somewhat muted.
- Strength of evidence: the results in the paper leave me with certain questions (most notably #6 below) as to whether the authors have sufficiently validated their hypothesized effect.
- Implications: the paper enumerates a number of interesting facts (e.g. dependence on $\epsilon$) but doesn't really dive deeper beyond some of those facts, leaving the reader wondering as to why those facts were presented.

Overall, I enjoyed reading this paper. I don't think it's super noteworthy in terms of immediate impact, but it is (as far as I can tell) a novel discovery that may serve as a waypoint on our path to better understanding of oddball generalization phenomena in deep learning.
reviews.questions:
1. Is there a reason validation error/loss is not depicted in Fig 1? Given the emphasis in this paper on generalization, it seems odd not to depict in the headline figure.
2. Figure 2(c) looks either incorrect or misgenerated—the validation loss should not be zero at the beginning of training, unless I am missing something.
3. Do the authors have any explanation for why Figure 3(d) doesn't show a high-curvature update on the second plateau?
4. Can the authors elaborate more on the implications of the larger $\epsilon$ result? To me, larger $\epsilon$ ruining the grokking effect sort of implies that non-adaptive methods (e.g. plain GD) don't benefit from slingshotting either (which is exactly what the authors found empirically). But it would still be good to explicitly connect the dots here.
5. I am also left wondering about the result on the paragraph starting on line 243. It's cool that the authors discovered it but what is it supposed to tell me? What about low-dim data makes the slingshot effect less likely to occur?
6. Finally, is it fair to call this effect cyclic when most empirical examples only have two cycles (with a few having 3)? Is there a setup where we can see $n$ distinct plateaus and sharp norm increases for some $n >> 3$?
reviews.limitations:
The authors do a good job not overclaiming, and they explicitly enumerate a number of other regimes that achieve the results despite lack of "slingshot". Certain items are left unaddressed (see "Questions").

Minor
----

- Line 84: missing author name
- Line 112: what is "Adam without momentum" and how is it different from RMSProp?
- Line 145: "layers" $\rightarrow$ "layer"
- Line 170: "has" $\rightarrow$ "have"
- Line 245: "choices" $\rightarrow$ "choice"
- Line 254: "it's" $\rightarrow$ "its"
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Review for “Slingshot Dynamics in Adaptive Optimization”

Summary  
This paper presents a thorough empirical investigation into an intriguing cyclic behavior—termed the “slingshot” phenomenon—in adaptive optimizers such as Adam. Through a series of controlled experiments, the authors uncover oscillatory patterns in gradient norms, sharpness measures, and loss landscapes, and put forward a hypothesis linking these cycles to implicit regularization and delayed generalization effects. The work is commendable for its clear focus on empirical evidence rather than dense theoretical derivations. However, questions remain about the practical relevance of the slingshot effect under realistic training regimes, the depth of the causal analysis, and the overall strength and generality of the evidence.

Strengths  
1. Well‐Executed Empirical Study: The paper delivers a carefully designed suite of experiments that convincingly reveal a novel cyclic training dynamic in popular adaptive optimizers.  
2. Slingshot Hypothesis Supported: The preliminary data—particularly the alignment of sharpness spikes with loss reductions—lends credence to the authors’ interpretation of a “slingshot” mechanism.  
3. Empirical over Theoretical: By avoiding heavy, poorly motivated mathematical derivations, the manuscript remains focused and transparent, allowing readers to gauge the phenomena directly from the presented figures.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Practical Impact: The experiments rely heavily on unregularized settings. It is unclear whether the slingshot effect persists—or even matters—in the presence of standard regularization techniques (weight decay, dropout) used in practical deep learning.  
2. Strength of Evidence: Although suggestive, many observations lack sufficient validation. For instance, most runs exhibit only two or three cycles, calling into question the generality of a truly “cyclic” phenomenon.  
3. Surface‐Level Exploration: Key factors such as the role of the ε hyperparameter and the behavior on low‐dimensional synthetic data are enumerated but not deeply analyzed, leaving readers without insight into underlying causes or broader implications.  
4. Unaddressed Key Points: The manuscript does not quantify the total number of slingshot cycles achievable, nor does it explain why low-dimensional tasks dampen the effect. These omissions weaken the claim of a robust cyclic mechanism.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Validation Curves: Incorporate validation loss and accuracy trajectories into Figure 1 to directly assess the impact of slingshot cycles on generalization.  
2. Figure 2(c) Correction: Regenerate or correct this panel so the validation loss does not erroneously start at zero; the current plot undermines confidence in the accuracy of the curves.  
3. Clarify Figure 3(d): Offer an explanation for the missing high‐curvature update on the second plateau. Is this an artifact of the setup or a genuine limiter of the slingshot effect?  
4. ε Hyperparameter Analysis: Expand the discussion on how varying ε both suppresses the slingshot phenomenon and eliminates grokking, and draw connections to behavior in non‐adaptive optimizers.  
5. Low‐Dimensional Data Effects: Provide a detailed account of why the slingshot cycles are attenuated in low-dimensional synthetic experiments, and discuss the broader implications for real‐world tasks.  
6. More Cycles Demonstration: If the term “cyclic” is to be upheld, include one or two examples showing more than two or three complete slingshot phases, perhaps by adjusting learning rates, batch sizes, or model complexity.

Minor Comments  
- Figure 1 currently omits validation loss/accuracy despite emphasizing generalization throughout the text.  
- In Figure 2(c), the validation loss curve is offset incorrectly at the origin.  
- Figure 3(d) lacks the expected curvature spike on the second plateau; please verify the plotting code or experiment settings.  
- Typographical issues: missing author name in the related work, inconsistent use of “its” vs. “it’s,” and occasional singular/plural mismatches.

Conclusion  
Overall, this manuscript offers a technically sound and well‐executed empirical study of a previously undocumented training phenomenon. While the paper’s moderate‐to‐high impact is evident, its practical significance and mechanistic depth would benefit from additional validation, clearer plots, and deeper causal analysis. I therefore recommend a weak accept, contingent on the authors addressing the above concerns to strengthen the robustness and clarity of their claims.

Rating: 6 (Weak Accept)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors describe a "slingshot" effect present in the use of Adam (and cousins). This purportedly cyclic effect can be observed when the norm of the final layer sharply increases, often accompanied by an increase in train loss. The authors offer the slingshot effect as a possible explanation for the ability of Adam to induce generalization even without explicit regularization.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
This is overall a nicely executed empirical paper that reveals a moderately interesting phenomenon. A cyclic regime of generalization would be quite remarkable and this paper offers preliminary evidence that supports the authors' hypothesis. It is also refreshing that the authors did not attempt to handwave nonsense poorly-motivated math into the paper, which is unfortunately a severe problem even in 2022.

My main concerns are the following:

- Significance: Given that this effect explains generalization in unregularized optimization, the overall impact to practical deep learning is somewhat muted.
- Strength of evidence: the results in the paper leave me with certain questions (most notably #6 below) as to whether the authors have sufficiently validated their hypothesized effect.
- Implications: the paper enumerates a number of interesting facts (e.g. dependence on $\epsilon$) but doesn't really dive deeper beyond some of those facts, leaving the reader wondering as to why those facts were presented.

Overall, I enjoyed reading this paper. I don't think it's super noteworthy in terms of immediate impact, but it is (as far as I can tell) a novel discovery that may serve as a waypoint on our path to better understanding of oddball generalization phenomena in deep learning.

Questions:
1. Is there a reason validation error/loss is not depicted in Fig 1? Given the emphasis in this paper on generalization, it seems odd not to depict in the headline figure.
2. Figure 2(c) looks either incorrect or misgenerated—the validation loss should not be zero at the beginning of training, unless I am missing something.
3. Do the authors have any explanation for why Figure 3(d) doesn't show a high-curvature update on the second plateau?
4. Can the authors elaborate more on the implications of the larger $\epsilon$ result? To me, larger $\epsilon$ ruining the grokking effect sort of implies that non-adaptive methods (e.g. plain GD) don't benefit from slingshotting either (which is exactly what the authors found empirically). But it would still be good to explicitly connect the dots here.
5. I am also left wondering about the result on the paragraph starting on line 243. It's cool that the authors discovered it but what is it supposed to tell me? What about low-dim data makes the slingshot effect less likely to occur?
6. Finally, is it fair to call this effect cyclic when most empirical examples only have two cycles (with a few having 3)? Is there a setup where we can see $n$ distinct plateaus and sharp norm increases for some $n >> 3$?

Limitations:
The authors do a good job not overclaiming, and they explicitly enumerate a number of other regimes that achieve the results despite lack of "slingshot". Certain items are left unaddressed (see "Questions").

Minor
----

- Line 84: missing author name
- Line 112: what is "Adam without momentum" and how is it different from RMSProp?
- Line 145: "layers" $\rightarrow$ "layer"
- Line 170: "has" $\rightarrow$ "have"
- Line 245: "choices" $\rightarrow$ "choice"
- Line 254: "it's" $\rightarrow$ "its"

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents an empirical study on an optimization anomaly, which is referred to as the Slingshot Mechanism/Effect by the authors, when adaptive optimizers (e.g., Adams) are used for solving supervised learning problems. Specifically, it was found that, when adaptive optimizers were used, cyclic phase transitions between stable and unstable training occurred and were correlated with how the norm of the last-layer weights changed. The authors also reported findings on the correlations between the onset of the Slingshot Mechanism and the glokking phenomenon.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

* The reported findings hint that extra care may be required when adaptive optimizers are used. The results may also promote further theoretical studies on the limitations of using adaptive optimizers in supervised learning.

Weaknesses:

* Additional numerical experiments could have been conducted to help future theorists better understand the source of issues. In particular, some rather simple diagnoses could have been done but were missing from the paper.
* There is much room for improvement in presentation of the paper. See the specific comments in the Questions section.
reviews.questions:
**Is the reported phenomenon unique to "deep models"?**

I did not see any numerical experiments on "shallow models." From the appendix, I only see experiments with a transformer (12 layers and 10 million parameters), a CNN with VGG-like architecture (which I assume to be a deep model), a deep MLP with 6 layers, and a deep linear model with 6 layers. Without experiments with simple/shallow models, it is premature to draw the conclusion that Slingshot is a general phenomenon as the authors claimed in Lines 154-162. 

As a matter of fact, given that the authors tried to illustrate the phenomenon using simple quadratic functions (Line 174), could the authors conduct a set of numerical experiments using quadratic functions to see whether the phenomenon still persists?

**Some simple numerical diagnoses could have been done.**

As evident from Line 178, one issue with the adaptive optimization schemes considered is that the effective step size $\mu / (|g| + \epsilon)$ can be much larger than the unnormalized step size $\mu$ when the magnitude of the gradient $g$ is small and $\epsilon$ is small. (Here, a small $\epsilon$ is assumed; otherwise, the update rule will not adapt much to the magnitude of $g$.) This implies that, when the magnitude of $g$ is small, i.e., when the current iterate is near a stationary point, the next iterate of the gradient-based update can be very far from the stationary point, potentially leading to a sudden increase in the loss. 

For gradient-based updates, when the objective function is smooth, it is known that a sufficient condition for decrease is to choose the step size to be less than $2/L$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of the gradient (and is related to the Hessian). It would be useful to plot in Figure 3 the effective step size, given by $\mu / (|g| + \epsilon)$, along with the sharpness to see if the step size is still small enough for the sufficient condition for decrease to hold. If the violation of the condition coincides with the increase in the loss, then this would provide a simple explanation of the Slingshot Effect.

**Comments on presentation and typos:**

* Are the Slingshot Mechanism and the Slingshot Effect the same thing? Both terms are used throughout the paper.
* Figure 1 and its first citation are too far apart: Figure 1 appears on the first page (even before the main text), but it citation does not appear until Line 158. 
* A typo in Line 190: "...evidence of **Elingshot** Effects."
* Line 230: What is $3.10e^{-04}4$? Also, it uncommon to write $10^{-04}$ instead of $10^{-4}$. This has occurred several times throughout the paper.
reviews.limitations: I do not have any comments on this.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents an empirical study on an optimization anomaly, which is referred to as the Slingshot Mechanism/Effect by the authors, when adaptive optimizers (e.g., Adams) are used for solving supervised learning problems. Specifically, it was found that, when adaptive optimizers were used, cyclic phase transitions between stable and unstable training occurred and were correlated with how the norm of the last-layer weights changed. The authors also reported findings on the correlations between the onset of the Slingshot Mechanism and the glokking phenomenon.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

* The reported findings hint that extra care may be required when adaptive optimizers are used. The results may also promote further theoretical studies on the limitations of using adaptive optimizers in supervised learning.

Weaknesses:

* Additional numerical experiments could have been conducted to help future theorists better understand the source of issues. In particular, some rather simple diagnoses could have been done but were missing from the paper.
* There is much room for improvement in presentation of the paper. See the specific comments in the Questions section.

Questions:
**Is the reported phenomenon unique to "deep models"?**

I did not see any numerical experiments on "shallow models." From the appendix, I only see experiments with a transformer (12 layers and 10 million parameters), a CNN with VGG-like architecture (which I assume to be a deep model), a deep MLP with 6 layers, and a deep linear model with 6 layers. Without experiments with simple/shallow models, it is premature to draw the conclusion that Slingshot is a general phenomenon as the authors claimed in Lines 154-162. 

As a matter of fact, given that the authors tried to illustrate the phenomenon using simple quadratic functions (Line 174), could the authors conduct a set of numerical experiments using quadratic functions to see whether the phenomenon still persists?

**Some simple numerical diagnoses could have been done.**

As evident from Line 178, one issue with the adaptive optimization schemes considered is that the effective step size $\mu / (|g| + \epsilon)$ can be much larger than the unnormalized step size $\mu$ when the magnitude of the gradient $g$ is small and $\epsilon$ is small. (Here, a small $\epsilon$ is assumed; otherwise, the update rule will not adapt much to the magnitude of $g$.) This implies that, when the magnitude of $g$ is small, i.e., when the current iterate is near a stationary point, the next iterate of the gradient-based update can be very far from the stationary point, potentially leading to a sudden increase in the loss. 

For gradient-based updates, when the objective function is smooth, it is known that a sufficient condition for decrease is to choose the step size to be less than $2/L$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of the gradient (and is related to the Hessian). It would be useful to plot in Figure 3 the effective step size, given by $\mu / (|g| + \epsilon)$, along with the sharpness to see if the step size is still small enough for the sufficient condition for decrease to hold. If the violation of the condition coincides with the increase in the loss, then this would provide a simple explanation of the Slingshot Effect.

**Comments on presentation and typos:**

* Are the Slingshot Mechanism and the Slingshot Effect the same thing? Both terms are used throughout the paper.
* Figure 1 and its first citation are too far apart: Figure 1 appears on the first page (even before the main text), but it citation does not appear until Line 158. 
* A typo in Line 190: "...evidence of **Elingshot** Effects."
* Line 230: What is $3.10e^{-04}4$? Also, it uncommon to write $10^{-04}$ instead of $10^{-4}$. This has occurred several times throughout the paper.

Limitations:
I do not have any comments on this.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents an empirical investigation of what the authors term the “Slingshot Mechanism,” a phenomenon whereby adaptive optimizers (e.g., Adam) exhibit sudden loss spikes and trajectory reversals when gradients become very small. The key insight is that the effective step size μ/(|g|+ε) can far exceed the classical gradient‐descent stability bound 2/L as |g|→0, leading to instability and sharp jumps in loss and model sharpness. The study provides compelling deep‐network experiments (ResNet, Vision Transformer) that illuminate this behavior and cautions practitioners that adaptive methods may require extra care.  

Strengths  
1. The empirical findings are novel and important: they highlight a subtle failure mode of widely used adaptive optimizers, namely that extremely small gradients can produce oversized updates.  
2. By drawing attention to this Slingshot phenomenon, the paper opens the door for further theoretical work on the limitations and stability properties of adaptive methods in supervised learning.  

Weaknesses  
1. Claims of broad generality are premature: all experiments are on deep architectures, so it is unclear whether shallow networks or simple quadratic objectives exhibit the same behavior.  
2. The lack of minimal diagnostic tests—such as a quadratic loss L(x)=½xᵀAx+Bᵀx+C—limits our understanding of whether the mechanism is intrinsic to adaptive updates or arises only in complex, nonconvex landscapes.  
3. Presentation issues distract from the technical message: inconsistent terminology (“Slingshot Mechanism” vs. “Slingshot Effect”), misplaced figure citations, typographical errors (e.g., “Elingshot Effects”), and nonstandard scientific notation (e.g., 10^{-4} vs. 10⁻⁴).  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Run experiments on shallow models (e.g., a two‐layer network) and on simple convex quadratics to test whether Slingshot transitions persist in these settings.  
2. In Figure 3, overlay the effective step size μ/(|g|+ε) with a plot of trajectory sharpness (uᵀHu/‖u‖²) to verify violations of the step‐size bound 2/L at each loss spike.  
3. Clearly state whether “Slingshot Mechanism” and “Slingshot Effect” refer to the same phenomenon, and use one term consistently throughout.  
4. Move figure citations to the sentences in which the figures are first discussed.  
5. Correct typos, standardize notation (e.g., write ε as a subscript or superscript), and adopt uniform scientific notation.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This work is technically sound and offers valuable empirical evidence about a previously underappreciated instability in adaptive optimizers. While the limited scope of experiments prevents strong claims of generality, the insights provided are likely to stimulate both practical caution and theoretical follow-up. I therefore recommend a borderline accept (Rating: 5).
